EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough moving through South Florida and a sea breeze boundary produced strong thunderstorms over Palm Beach County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued over most of the interior and southwest sections of South Florida during January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall during January led to the continuation of drought conditions across the area. Glades County was in an extreme drought (D3) status, while Hendry, western Palm Beach and northern Collier counties were in a severe drought (D2) status. ||Rainfall amounts during January ranged from 0.5 to 1.0 inches over coastal and inland Collier, Hendry and Glades counties to between 1.0 and 1.5 inches near the south and east shores of Lake Okeechobee. The level of Lake Okeechobee remained at just over 10 feet above sea level, which was over 4 feet below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an approaching surface front over Georgia, passing upper level energy per an upper level trough digging over the eastern seaboard and high atmospheric moisture and instability fueled numerous severe storms in the afternoon and evening along the sea breezes and resultant storm outflows under a prevailing southwest flow regime.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down across Bunnell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area Tuesday night with strong high pressure behind the front bringing an arctic airmass over the state on strong northerly flow. Freezing temperatures settled into Levy county Wednesday morning and then settled across the entire area for Thursday morning, with many locations seeing freezing temperatures for six or more hours. North winds of 10 to 15 mph Thursday morning also allowed wind chill values to fall to 15 to 20 degrees across the area. Temperatures moderated for Friday morning, but freezing temperatures were still felt across the Nature Coast for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing conditions were felt across much of the county, with the COOP station near Avon Park reaching a low of 28 degrees, and the COOP in Archbold reaching 29 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convective complex over Georgia initiated by a passing upper level short wave early in the day produced a large scale outflow boundary that advanced over our zones during the afternoon and early evening hours over a moist and unstable airmass. Outflows combined with a strong sea breeze collision near the east coast ignited scattered severe storms across the area.

Glades County was in an extreme drought (D3) status, while Hendry, western Palm Beach and northern Collier counties were in a severe drought (D2) status. ||Rainfall amounts during January ranged from 0.5 to 1.0 inches over coastal and inland Collier, Hendry and Glades counties to between 1.0 and 1.5 inches near the south and east shores of Lake Okeechobee. The level of Lake Okeechobee remained at just over 10 feet above sea level, which was over 4 feet below normal.

An arctic cold front moved through South Florida late on New Year's night, with the coldest temperatures in 5 years experienced across parts of the area on the morning of January 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing temperatures affected most of southwest and interior South Florida on the morning of January 3rd. Lowest temperatures in Glades County were 28 degrees in Palmdale, 29 degrees in Ortona and 30 degrees in Moore Haven. ||Despite the freezing temperatures, crop damage was reported as minimal due to minimal duration of temperatures at or below 28 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved through South Florida late on New Year's night, with the coldest temperatures in 5 years experienced across parts of the area on the morning of January 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing temperatures affected most of southwest and interior South Florida on the morning of January 3rd. Lowest official temperatures in Hendry County were 29 degrees at Big Cypress, 30 degrees in Clewiston and 31 in Devils Garden. Unofficial low temperatures at some farms were as low as 27 degrees. ||Damage to crops was noted as limited, and mostly to tomatoes and peppers. Estimated losses in most farms was estimated at 10 to 20 percent, with as much as 40 percent estimated at one farm near the Collier/Hendry County line. Much of the damage was due to the strong winds which accompanied the freezing temperatures overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure built into the area with strong winds fanning several wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds and low relative humidity values rekindled a woods fire which then rapidly spread destroying a mobile home on Knollwood Road off U.S. Highway 17 in Southern Clay County. Due to the wind and heavily wooded area fire units from Clay, Putnam, and St Johns Counties and tractors from the Florida Division of Forestry fought the fire. The mobile home was reported to be a total loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad mean layer trough was over the southeast region with a ridge axis across central Florida. An active southwest flow regime tracked the west coast sea breeze inland during the afternoon where it merged with the east coast in the late afternoon and early evening across eastern Florida. High instability and a passing upper level short wave enhanced a few severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on power lines near Salt Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary was positioned over Southeast Georgia with a moist and unstable airmass across the local area in advance of the front. Precipitable water amounts of 2.12 inches were in place and very high surface based instability index values with CAPEs around 3500 J/kg and lifted index values of -9 to -10.  Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the area during the afternoon and evening along the approaching front , sea breezes and old outflow boundaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the Villas at Ponte Vedra Beach and caused a fire that damaged 19 townhouse rooms in a building. The time of the event was based on lightning data plots. The cost of damaged was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad mean layer trough was over the southeast region with a ridge axis across central Florida. An active southwest flow regime tracked the west coast sea breeze inland during the afternoon where it merged with the east coast in the late afternoon and early evening across eastern Florida. High instability and a passing upper level short wave enhanced a few severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was observed near Armstrong.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Morning soundings indicated a moist and unstable airmass over the local area with low wind shear and a light southwest steering flow. The precipitable water was 1.76 inches at Jacksonville with around 3000 J/kg of CAPE and a lifted index of -6 degrees Celcius. An upper level low pressure system approached the eastern seaboard during the afternoon which pushed a surface trough over Georgia. This trough in concert with the sea breezes sparked numerous severe storms across the local forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was observed at Nelson's Outdoor Resort on Southeast Highway 42. Hail cracked the windshield of a car. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid and upper level trough extended from the Great Lakes south across the lower Mississippi River Valley and a weak shortwave trough was offshore of the southeast U.S. Atlantic Coast. A surface ridge axis was across central Florida. The Jacksonville sounding depicted a precipitable water of over 2 inches and modified afternoon CAPEs were around 2500 J/kg with a lifted index of -10 and 500 mb temperatures of -10 degrees Celcius. Scattered severe storms developed across the area during the afternoon and evening when a passing upper level short wave trough vorticity maxima traversed the moist and unstable airmass as the sea breezes developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down in Orange Park and around 4,000 customers were without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions during the month of May led to a reinstatement of severe drought conditions over southwest Florida and the Lake Okeechobee region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions which began in mid-April continued through May caused rapidly drying soil conditions across southwest Florida and the Lake Okeechobee region. This led to a reinstatement of severe drought (D2) conditions across these areas. Rainfall totals for the month of May ranged from around a third to a half of an inch at Naples, Marco Island and Ochopee to around one and one half inches at Clewiston and Moore Haven, to over 3 inches at Immokalee. The dry soil and lack of rainfall contributed to several large wildfires which affected the area.||The level of Lake Okeechobee dropped to 9.65 inches by the end of the month, it's lowest level since the dry season began last fall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An increasingly moist and unstable airmass combined with upper level energy in advance of a remnant MCS complex over Georgia spawned scattered severe storms across the forecast area during the afternoon and evening. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mean 1000-500 mb layer trough was offshore of the Florida Atlantic coast. Drier, northerly flow limited convection over coastal southeast Georiga, but high precipitable water values lingered over central Florida with values around 2 inches. Modified sounding profiles indicated afternoon CAPEs of 3700 J/kg and enhanced the likelihood of strong winds in afternoon convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred due to poor drainage on 105th Street between Jammes and Blanding Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of muck fires burned along the dry lakebed of southwestern Lake Okeechobee near the Glades and Hendry county shoreline for almost the entire month of May. Smoke from these fires periodically closed area roads and caused an accident involving fatalities in northern Hendry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wildfires burning muck or peat exposed by ongoing dry conditions along the southwestern shore of Lake Okeechobee spread to over 15 miles long from near Lakeport to near Clewiston and covered almost 30,000 acres as of May 31. Multiple road closures occurred nearly every night through the period, especially along US 27 and state roads 720 and 78, as the smoke settled near the ground and caused low visibilities. Several RVs were also threatened near the Moore Haven Marina & RV Resort.||The very low visibilities from the wildfires caused a chain-reaction accident on the morning of May 16th along US Highway 27 at Airglades Boulevard in extreme northern Hendry County between Clewiston and Moore Haven. A total of five accidents were reported, with 11 vehicles involved. One person died and 29 were taken to the hospital. The lead vehicle in the crash failed to slow down to avoid a collision, which triggered the rest of the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Morning soundings indicated a moist and unstable airmass over the local area with low wind shear and a light southwest steering flow. The precipitable water was 1.76 inches at Jacksonville with around 3000 J/kg of CAPE and a lifted index of -6 degrees Celcius. An upper level low pressure system approached the eastern seaboard during the afternoon which pushed a surface trough over Georgia. This trough in concert with the sea breezes sparked numerous severe storms across the local forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were blown down and wind were estimated at 60 mph at Nelson's Outdoor Resort. Vehicles had minor damage from fallen branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave upper level trough passed over the area during the late afternoon and evening hours around the northeast periphery of an upper level ridge to our west. The storm likely initiated along the sea breeze or an outflow over a moist and unstable airmass near a lingering surface trough that was over north Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported nickel size hail and wind gusts to 47 mph. Minor damage was observed with localized flooding near Godbold Avenue and Satellite Lane.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public measured 5.5 inches of rainfall within 2 hours from 2 different rain gauges about 1,000 feet apart. A few minutes of small hail was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area Tuesday night with strong high pressure behind the front bringing an arctic airmass over the state on strong northerly flow. Freezing temperatures settled into Levy county Wednesday morning and then settled across the entire area for Thursday morning, with many locations seeing freezing temperatures for six or more hours. North winds of 10 to 15 mph Thursday morning also allowed wind chill values to fall to 15 to 20 degrees across the area. Temperatures moderated for Friday morning, but freezing temperatures were still felt across the Nature Coast for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Winter Haven and the AWOS at Lakeland both had a wind chill value of 17 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of dry weather promoted dangerous fire conditions across inland North Florida. A wildfire was first reported by Gilchrist Emergency Management around 5:20 pm EDT on April 30th, and this fire was located approximately 4 miles east-northeast of Trenton. At the time of the first report, the cause of the fire was unknown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire was reported by Gilchrist County Emergency Management around 5:20 pm EDT. At the time, no homes were threatened.  Tyler Grade Road was closed.  By 11:31 pm EDT, the fire was 1,000 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent onshore flow caused significant beach erosion in Ponte Vedra Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several days of high astronomical tides combined with persistent onshore flow and rough surf caused significant beach erosion near the 3500 block of South Ponte Vedra Beach Blvd. Several homes are reported as being threatened with collapse. This area has historically suffered from persistent beach erosion with significant erosion events also occurring in September and October of last year. The cost of damage and repair was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north of Florida early in the day which increased surface based instability and low level moisture. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny size hail north of Raiford. The time of the report was estimated based on Doppler radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued into April across interior and western portions of South Florida, although beneficial rains early in the month were enough to remove the severe drought conditions (D2) from South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued into the first few days of April across interior and western sections of South Florida. However, heavy rains during the first week of April dropped from 2 to 5 inches of rain across the area. As a result, Glades, Hendry and Collier counties were dropped from the severe drought (D2) status. ||The rains also brought the level of Lake Okeechobee up to 10.5 feet. Nevertheless, water usage restrictions continued in effect throughout the month across interior and southwest Florida.||Total agricultural damage estimates from the severe drought which began in March 2007 are estimated to be near $1 billion in the state of Florida, with much of that in South Florida.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued into March across interior and western portions of South Florida, although some improvement in drought conditions was noted by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions persisted across interior and western sections of South Florida in March, which is typically one of the driest months of the year. However, several strong mid and upper level disturbances moved across the area which provided beneficial rains over some areas, primarily around and over Lake Okeechobee. ||Areas around Lake Okeechobee received between 4 and 6 inches of rain in March, which is above the normal monthly amount. As a result, Glades County went from an extreme (D3) drought status to a severe (D2) drought status. Western Palm Beach County went from a severe (D2) drought status to a moderate (D1) status. The level of Lake Okeechobee rose slightly during the month, reaching 10.2 feet by the end of March.||Collier County received less rain than areas around Lake Okeechobee, with monthly amounts ranging from 0.70 inches at Naples to 2.86 inches at Immokalee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short-lived tornado, not associated with a supercell thunderstorm, occurred along the leading edge of a squall line across the Flamingo Island subdivision of Marathon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado, possibly a landfalling waterspout, produced EF0 damage along an intermittent path from Saguro Lane through Ocatillo Lane in the Flamingo Island subdivision of Marathon. The longest continuous damage path was approximately 100 yards long with a width of 25 yards. Damage was limited to live palm fronds and small broken branches, several found hanging in utility wires. Three homes were reported with minor roof damage, mainly to soffets blown upward into the roof cavity and a few shingles torn off. A second damage path, in-line with the path in Flamingo Island, was noted at the Marathon High School. One small tree was broken at the base, with an unsecured soccer goal overturned. A wooden canopy used as a sunscreen for the high school athletic field's press box was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain bands from Tropical Storm Fay moving onshore from the Atlantic produced a tornado in Stuart.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado associated with a rain band moving on shore from Tropical Storm Fay, touched down briefly in Stuart. The tornado overturned a panel truck and damaged a service station awning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over northern Palm Beach on the evening on June 26th caused lightning to strike a car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car was struck by lightning while driving on I-95 in northern Palm Beach County between Indiantown and Donald Ross roads. A female passenger was taken to the hospital for precationary measures. The vehicle was inoperable, likely due to electrical damage caused by the lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions persisted through June over much of southwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions which started in April continued into much of June. Typical summer rainfall was slow to arrive to southwest Florida. As a result, the level of Lake Okeechobee reached a yearly low of 9.28 in mid-June before rising back up to almost 9.5 feet by the end of June as rains finally arrived.||Monthly observed rainfall totals varied widely from area to area, with a low of 3.81 at Raccoon Point in eastern Collier County to a high of 14.28 at Devils Garden in Hendry County. Naples received a total of 9.45 inches, most of this in the second half of June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large wildfire which began in mid-May burned over the Everglades of western Miami-Dade County until it was declared 100% contained as of June 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mustang Corner Fire started on May 14th and continued |until June 4th when it was declared 100% contained. The fire burned a total of 39,465 acres in the Everglades National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued into July over the Lake Okeechobee area, but heavy rains during the month of July brought an end to the drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions which have persisted for most of the year lingered into the first part of July. Heavy rains throughout the month, combined with rising lake and river levels, enabled the area to be downgraded from a severe drought (D2) to a moderate drought status (D1). Rainfall totals around the area were mainly in the 8 to 11 inch range, which is several inches above normal for July. The level of Lake Okeechobee rose over a foot during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of muck fires which started in May continued to burn into June along the dry lakebed of southwestern Lake Okeechobee near the Glades and Hendry county shoreline. Smoke from these fires periodically closed area roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wildfires burning muck or peat exposed by ongoing dry conditions along the southwestern shore of Lake Okeechobee spread to over 15 miles long from near Lakeport to near Clewiston and covered almost 30,000 acres as of June 1. Multiple road closures occurred nearly every night through the period, especially along US 27 and state roads 720 and 78, as the smoke settled near the ground and caused low visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: East northeast steering flow around Tropical Storm Hanna prompted isolated shower and thunderstorm activity across the coastal waters.
Moderate low level northeast flow and strong jet dynamics favored training bands of coastal convection as a local Nor'Easter event established.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management relayed 24 hour rainfall amounts from 9/20 to 9/21/2008 of up to 5.30 inches in North Palm Coast. Northeast Palm Coast observed 4.40 inches of rainfall and Andulusia had 4.00 inches. Elsewhere across the eastern portions of the county rainfall amounts generally ranged from 2-4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonable upper level closed low positioned over South Georgia and a surface low over the Florida panhandle combined to bring two distinct storm environments capable of supporting supercells to the Jacksonville County Warning area through the day on October 9, 2008. The first round of convection affected mainly southeast Georgia where widespread discrete supercells produced large hail. During the afternoon, low level instability over north Florida supported stronger surface based storms in advance of the approaching surface low and its attendant cold front, and several of these storms produced significant rainfall amounts and downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported near Peanut Mills along SW Railroad Avenue.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two Walton County men were killed when lightning struck their fishing boat on Choctawhatchee Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several periods of strong onshore flow backed water into the St. Johns River over the course of a couple of weeks. As the river flowed back north into the Atlantic, a couple weeks of higher than normal astronomical tides caused flooding related problems along the river from Duval county to Putnam County in the Jacksonville County Warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: River flooding of the St. Johns River caused extreme flooding of Clarks Fishcamp and has prompted the business to close until further notice. Persistent and strong onshore flow for almost 2 weeks rose water levels in the river to about 2 to 4 feet above normal tidal levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered lines of showers and isolated thunderstorms were stretched from the SW to NE over the coastal waters. Low level onshore flow was observed with a very moist sounding and an upper level jet of 50-70 kts was oriented over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thin rope funnel cloud lasted about 5 minutes before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A early evening thunderstorm on the 21st felled numerous trees north of Tallahassee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous pine trees were toppled by winds near the Florida-Georgia state line just north of Iamonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A early evening thunderstorm on the 21st felled numerous trees north of Tallahassee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down just south of Maclay Gardens State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late night thunderstorm damaged a commercial building in Tallahassee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning damaged a commercial building at 2800 Remington Green Circle in northeast Tallahassee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms on the 25th knocked down several trees just east of Tallahassee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees fell on Victory Garden Drive which took down some power poles and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 56 year old male out of town visitor from Louisiana drowned in rough surf associated with Tropical Storm Edouard near Perdido Key. He was attempting to help another person that was in trouble when he drowned.
An afternoon sea breeze formed along the coast and combined with the warm and moist environment to allow scattered thunderstorms to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 3.63 inches of rain falling in one hour in Citrus Park and causing minor street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay blanketed most of the state of Florida with over 6 inches of rain.  Only the Tampa Bay Area escaped Fay's deluge with rainfall generally less than one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Southwest Florida Water Management District gauge recorded 8.62 inches of rain. However, radar estimated precipitation showed a small area of 10 to 14 inches near the intersection of Highway 60 and the Kissimmee River. Homes were flooded in this area. Radar showed 1 to 3 inches of rain in the far west part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay blanketed most of the state of Florida with over 6 inches of rain.  Only the Tampa Bay Area escaped Fay's deluge with rainfall generally less than one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer recorded 7.88 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay blanketed most of the state of Florida with over 6 inches of rain.  Only the Tampa Bay Area escaped Fay's deluge with rainfall generally less than one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Southwest Florida Water Management District gauge recorded 5.03 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay blanketed most of the state of Florida with over 6 inches of rain.  Only the Tampa Bay Area escaped Fay's deluge with rainfall generally less than one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer recorded 8.53 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Gustav moved west-northwest through the northwest Caribbean Sea, crossing over western Cuba before continuing into the Gulf of Mexico. Tropical storm force winds spread over the Middle and Lower Florida Keys of Monroe County August 30 through 31, 2008.  Storm total rainfall was very light, owing to the large distance between the Florida Keys and the core of the hurricane. There was minor tidal overwash from the Atlantic side of the Keys due to a minor storm surge and large seas in the Florida Straits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor overwash occurred along the south shore of Key West in association with the passage of Hurricane Gustav. Storm tides peaked at 2.5 feet above sea level.  Large waves resulted in inundation of several streets along the immediate shoreline.  No property damage resulted from the coastal flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay blanketed most of the state of Florida with over 6 inches of rain.  Only the Tampa Bay Area escaped Fay's deluge with rainfall generally less than one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Remote Automated Weather Station recorded 11.72 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread rainfall of 6 to 14 inches caused overland and river flooding from Fort Myers to Sebring and extreme western Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains of 6-14 inches associated with Tropical Storm Fay caused extensive sheet flow flooding from the Imperial River that runs through Bonita Springs. Flood damage was reported to numerous roads and homes with 500 residents evacuated to shelters.  The preliminary damage estimates included $250,000 to roads and $250,000 to homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread rainfall of 6 to 14 inches caused overland and river flooding from Fort Myers to Sebring and extreme western Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rains of 5-12 inches associated with Tropical Storm Fay caused $150,000 in flood damage to washed out roads and culverts. Five homes along Arbuckle Creek received flood damage totaling $70,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved southeast over the coastal waters and inland along the coastal counties. A severe thunderstorm wind gust occurred at one point along the line as it moved across the northern half of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees and power lines blown down in Inverness.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay blanketed most of the state of Florida with over 6 inches of rain.  Only the Tampa Bay Area escaped Fay's deluge with rainfall generally less than one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Southwest Florida Water Management District gauge recorded 3.86 inches of rain. However, radar estimated precipitation showed 6 to 8 inches in the southeast part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread rainfall of 6 to 14 inches caused overland and river flooding from Fort Myers to Sebring and extreme western Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread 6 to 8 inches of rain fell in the eastern third of the county.  Radar estimated rainfall of 14 inches that caused overland flooding just south of the intersection of Highway 60 and the Kissimmee River. Seven homes received up to 3 feet of water that caused $250,000 in damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Gustav moved west-northwest through the northwest Caribbean Sea, crossing over western Cuba before continuing into the Gulf of Mexico. Tropical storm force winds spread over the Middle and Lower Florida Keys of Monroe County August 30 through 31, 2008.  Storm total rainfall was very light, owing to the large distance between the Florida Keys and the core of the hurricane. There was minor tidal overwash from the Atlantic side of the Keys due to a minor storm surge and large seas in the Florida Straits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Gustav passed well southwest of the Florida Keys of Monroe County during the night from August 30 to 31, 2008. Tropical storm force winds affected the Middle and Lower Florida Keys, with the highest sustained wind in the Lower Florida Keys measured at 40 knots (46 mph) at Key West International Airport, with gusts to 48 knots (55 mph).  Storm total rainfall was less than two inches at all reporting locations in the Lower Florida Keys. The lowest sea level pressure was reported to be 1004.1 mb at Key West International Airport, and 1001.2 mb at sea at Pulaski Shoal Light, located about 7 miles east-northeast of Dry Tortugas National Park.  Coastal storm tides peaked at 2.1 feet above sea level at Key West Harbor, but were estimated at 2.5 feet above sea level along the south shore of Key West.  Property damage is unknown, due to blending of Monroe County damage assessments with Hurricane Ike, which affected the Keys less than two weeks later.  One direct fatality occurred at sea 20 miles south of Dry Tortugas... approximately 80 miles west southwest of Key West.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ike passed well south of the area, however high astronomical tide and surge from Ike brought high storm tides to the area.  The storm tide was higher with Ike than with Gustav in many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As hurricane Ike moved well south of the panhandle, storm surge caused major beach erosion across the area.  The astronomical tide was very high and the surge adding to this caused the total tide height to be higher than what occurred in Gustav.  The beaches also had very little time to recover from the battering waves of Gustav before Ike affected the area.  The beaches took a pounding from the waves and surge and the water washed across several places from Pensacola Beach to Destin.  Highway 98 that runs from Fort Walton Beach to Destin had to be closed because of high water.  Pensacola Beach also had water across low spots in the road that runs along the beach.  Like Gustav, some of the water actually got into the lower floors of some condos. Navarre Beach also took a pounding from waves. The higher than normal tides also impacted the north parts of the bays in northwest Florida.  Some flooding was reported in the north parts of Pensacola, Blackwater and Choctawhatchee Bays. Water never got into any homes but it got close.  The surge height was 2.9 feet in Pensacola with a storm tide height of 4.3 feet.  The surge height in Destin was 2.5 feet with a storm tide height of 4.0 feet. Total estimate damage from the surge was $1.2M.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a couple of homes near Pensacola.  One of the strikes injured a male.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home was hit by lightning and a fire started.  The home only suffered minor damage from the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wind gust from a thunderstorm was measured at 64 mph.  No damage was found in the area.

Heavy rains fell along the coast during the early morning hours causing street flooding.  Several roads were closed due to high water in the southwest part of the county. Rainfall estimates of 4-6 inches were received.
A northward moving boundary produced severe thunderstorms across central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a funnel cloud in the Campbell area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward moving boundary produced severe thunderstorms across central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement and the public reported a funnel cloud over the Atlantic off Melbourne Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate swell coming into East Central Florida produced rip currents in Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 45 year old man drowned in a rip current at Cape Canaveral.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sunny warm weather brought a lot of people to the beach in Brevard County. A moderate swell produced numerous rip currents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A moderate swell produced a high number of rip currents in Brevard County. Local life guards reported dozens of rescues in the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough over South Florida led to the formation of heavy showers and thunderstorms over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All floors of a five-story condominium building near Inverrary Country Club in Lauderhill flooded as a result of heavy rains during the evening of September 28. Roof drains backed up, resulting in water leaking through the roof and entering 15 apartment units via air-conditioning units.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough over South Florida led to the formation of heavy showers and thunderstorms over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An apartment at the Stonebridge Gardens complex in Lauderhill flooded as water leaked through an electrical conduit. Residents planned to stay with relatives later that evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly surface winds ahead of a cold front over north Florida, in combination with a mid-level shortwave trough moving through the area, caused heavy rains and flooding over parts of southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains over a period of 4 hours caused significant ponding of water and flooding along the Biscayne Boulevard corridor in North Miami Beach and Aventura. Significant flooding was reported from NE 150 Street north to NE 186 Street. Water entered a body shop in Aventura where a dozen cars and the office were inundated by over a foot of water. Water also entered county buses along Biscayne Boulevard in North Miami Beach. Poor drainage caused by clogged drains contributed to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A waterspout moved west across Garrison Bight and made landfall at the Sewage Treatment Facility for the City of Key West on Fleming Key.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout moved ashore, crossing through part of the Sewage Treatment Facility for the City of Key West.  No damage was reported as the tornado moved over land devoid of vegetation and over several evaporation tanks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ike moved west to west-northwest across eastern Cuba, just offshore the south central coast, and then crossed through western Cuba into the Gulf of Mexico.  Tropical storm force winds and two tornadoes were reported in the Florida Keys. However, rainfall amounts and storm surge were minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ike passed well southwest of the Florida Keys and into the Gulf of Mexico. However, Ike's large wind field produced tropical storm force winds during September 9-10, 2008.  Storm total rainfall in the Monroe County Upper Keys was less than two inches.  The highest sustained wind of 37knots with gusts to 53 knots was recorded at an automated sensor on Upper Matecumbe Key. Coastal storm tides were estimated up to 2 feet above sea level on the Atlantic shore of Lower Matecumbe Key. Two tornadoes were confirmed in the Monroe County Upper Keys at Lower Matecumbe Key and North Key Largo. No fatalities or injuries were reported in the Lower Keys of Monroe County. Mandatory evacuations for residents and visitors were ordered from September 6 through 7, 2008. Total damage to government property in Monroe County was estimated at $13.5 million dollars.  Approximately $1.2 million damage in debris removal and erosion occurred county-wide due to storm surge flooding, with $2.6 million estimated from combined effects from wind and wind-blown rain.  Of particular note was an estimated $7 million loss to the commercial lobster fishery due to lost and relocated traps.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A waterspout over Lower Sugarloaf Channel moved west and made landfall at a residential property in Bay Point.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout moved ashore along Bay Drive in Bay Point, overturning potted plants and trash cans.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continuation of moderate to strong south to southeast winds produced more rip currents along the southeast Florida beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rip currents nearly drowned a 62-year-old woman visiting from Montreal. The woman was swimming behind the Ocean Colony Condominiums in Lauderdale-By-The-Sea when she was caught in a rip current. A resident of the condominium complex ran out to the beach and rescued the woman. She was attended to by rescue personnel on shore, but did not go to the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ike moved west to west-northwest across eastern Cuba, just offshore the south central coast, and then crossed through western Cuba into the Gulf of Mexico.  Tropical storm force winds and two tornadoes were reported in the Florida Keys. However, rainfall amounts and storm surge were minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ike passed well southwest of the Florida Keys and into the Gulf of Mexico. However, Ike's large wind field produced tropical storm force winds during September 9-10, 2008.  Storm total rainfall was highest in the Monroe County Middle Keys at Marathon Airport, where 2.47 inches was recorded.  The highest sustained wind of 44 knots with gusts to 55 knots was recorded at Long Key. The lowest sea-level pressure of 1005.4 mb was recorded at Marathon Airport.  Coastal storm tides measured at up to 1.1 feet above sea level at Vaca Key, equating to about one foot of storm surge. No fatalities or injuries were reported in the Lower Keys of Monroe County. Mandatory evacuations for residents and visitors were ordered from September 6 through 7, 2008. Total damage to government property in Monroe County was estimated at $13.5 million dollars.  Approximately $1.2 million damage in debris removal and erosion occurred county-wide due to storm surge flooding, with $2.6 million estimated from combined effects from wind and wind-blown rain.  Of particular note was an estimated $7 million loss to the commercial lobster fishery due to lost and relocated traps.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across northwest Florida producing strong winds that damaged metal storage buildings in Allentown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced gusty winds that damaged metal buildings in Allentown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Canadian high pressure airmass moved into the region behind a cold front and provided cold and breezy conditions for a couple of days. Freezing temperatures were felt across the Nature Coast each morning and record low temperatures occurred as far south as Highlands county. ||Levy County: Freezing temperatures were felt county-wide each morning with lows ranging from 24-30 degrees on the 19th and from 26 to 31 degrees on the 20th. The COOP station at Usher Tower near Chiefland had record lows each morning, with a low of 24 degrees on the 19th beating the previous low of 32 set in 2006, and a low of 26 degrees on the 20th tying the record also set in 1968.||Citrus County: Freezing temperatures occurred away from the coast, with a mesonet station recording a low of 27 degrees on the 19th. ||Hernando County: Temperatures just touched freezing at the COOP station at Brooksville Chin Hill on the 19th. The low of 32 degrees set a new record low temperature, beating the previous low of 33 degrees set in 1951. Temperatures then dropped on the 20th with lows of 28 degrees at the Brooksville Hernando County Airport ASOS, and 26 degrees at the FAWN station in Brooksville. ||Sumter County: Temperatures did not fall below freezing on the 19th. On the 20th, the low at the COOP station in Bushnell was 29 degrees and this set a new record. The previous record was 30 degrees set in 1951.||Manatee County: The COOP station in Bradenton had a record low temperature on the 19th of 41 degrees, beating the previous low of 42 degrees set in 1970. Then on the 20th, the Sarasota-Bradenton International Airport ASOS had a record low of 39 degrees, beating the previous record of 40 degrees set in 1968.||Sarasota County: The COOP station in Venice had a record low temperature on the 20th of 39 degrees. The previous low was 42 degrees set in 1968.||Highlands County: A low temperature of 40 degrees on the 19th set a new record at the COOP station near Avon Park. The previous record was 41 degrees set in 2006. On the 20th, the low at the COOP station near Avon Park was again 40 degrees, tying the record also set in 1968. The COOP station at Archbold had a record low of 31 degrees on the 20th, beating the previous record of 38 degrees set in 2006.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing temperatures were felt county-wide with lows ranging from 24-30 degrees. The COOP station at Usher Tower near Chiefland had a record low of 24 degrees beating the previous low of 32 set in 2006.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 50 knots (58 mph) was measured atop the radiosonde launch facility at the National Weather Service Forecast Office Key West.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay moved north from Cuba, through the Florida Straits, and passed north through the Lower Florida Keys during the evening of August 18, 2008.  Fay produced tropical storm force winds, two tornadoes and heavy rainfall in the Florida Keys, however storm surge impact was minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coastal flooding as result of a high tide after the passage of Tropical Storm Fay resulted in damage to Sombrero Beach Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonable upper level closed low positioned over South Georgia and a surface low over the Florida panhandle combined to bring two distinct storm environments capable of supporting supercells to the Jacksonville County Warning area through the day on October 9, 2008. The first round of convection affected mainly southeast Georgia where widespread discrete supercells produced large hail. During the afternoon, low level instability over north Florida supported stronger surface based storms in advance of the approaching surface low and its attendant cold front, and several of these storms produced significant rainfall amounts and downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down near White Springs. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay moved north from Cuba, through the Florida Straits, and passed north through the Lower Florida Keys during the evening of August 18, 2008.  Fay produced tropical storm force winds, two tornadoes and heavy rainfall in the Florida Keys, however storm surge impact was minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay crossed through the Lower Florida Keys of Monroe County during the evening of August 18.  Across the Upper Florida Keys, storm total rainfall ranged from 3.42 inches at Plantation Key to 4.38 inches at Islamorada.  The highest sustained wind in the Upper Florida Keys of 32 knots (37 mph) with a peak gust of 46 knots (53 mph) was recorded at Upper Matecumbe Key. A peak gust of 52 knots (60 mph) was recorded at the Islamorada Fire Station. The lowest sea-level pressure was 997.1 mb was recorded at Cudjoe Key in the Lower Florida Keys.  Coastal storm tides across the Upper Florida Keys peaked at 2.5 feet above mean sea level along the south shore of Lower Matecumbe Key, equating to a storm surge less than one foot above astronomical tide levels. No injuries or fatalities were reported in the Upper Florida Keys of Monroe County.  25,000 people, mainly visitors and nonresidents of the Florida Keys, were evacuated. Countywide Tourism losses were estimated at 8 to 10 million dollars. Damage and preparedness costs to government facilities and infrastructure totaled 2.8 million dollars, with about one million dollars due to coastal flooding damage to roads, and 200,000 dollars due to erosion.  Approximately 120,000 dollars damaged occurred due to wind. 2 tornadoes occurred in the lower Keys, with no structural damage reported.  Total damage due to the tornadoes was approximately one thousand dollars.

During the afternoon, low level instability over north Florida supported stronger surface based storms in advance of the approaching surface low and its attendant cold front, and several of these storms produced significant rainfall amounts and downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported that 4 to 5 inches of rain had fallen with many streets flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout was observed southeast of Ramrod Key. The waterspout moved northwest and made landfall in the 400 block of West Indies Drive as an EF0 tornado. The tornado dissipated after crossing West Indies Drive almost immediately after landfall. No structural damage occurred, however a 4x4 post was sheared off at dock level.  The tornado also broke several large tree limbs, and removed a television cable service line from one residence. Estimated wind speed 60 to 70 mph per wind measurement of 62 mph coincident with no observed damage one half hour prior to the arrival of the tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in association with Tropical Storm Fay produced widespread street flooding in Key West. The rain-induced flooding caused a small section of tarmac at the Key West International Airport to collapse.  Rainfall amounts of 3.5 to 4.0 inches were reported in the Key West vicinity.  Heavier amounts up to 7.27 inches in Marathon and 7.05 inches in Ramrod Key resulted in temporary street and low area flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure off the North Carolina coast produced a long period swell on the east coast of Florida.  This created frequent rip currents that resulted in two fatalities and hundreds rip current rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 49 year old man drowned in a rip current at Hutchinson Island while rescuing his son from the rip current.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure off the North Carolina coast produced a long period swell on the east coast of Florida.  This created frequent rip currents that resulted in two fatalities and hundreds rip current rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 15 year old male drowned in a rip current at New Smyrna Beach. The Volusia County beach patrol reported over 200 people rescued from rip currents during this period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the area late in the evening on 12/1 and through the early morning hours on 12/2. Cold air settled into the Nature Coast through the day on 12/2 and allowed for several hours of sub-freezing temperatures on the morning of 12/3. ||Levy County experienced several hours of sub-freezing temperatures with lows from 27 to 31 degrees across the county. The lowest temperature of 27 degrees occurred at the cooperative station six miles southeast of Chiefland. ||Citrus County experienced over 9 hours of sub-freezing temperatures in some locations, with the lowest temperature of 25 degrees occurring at a mesonet station near Inverness.||Hernando County also had over 9 hours of sub-freezing temperatures with the lowest temperature of 24 degrees occurring at the FAWN station in Brooksville.||Sumter County had lows ranging from 28 to 32 degrees, with the lowest temperature of 28 degrees occurring at the cooperative station in Bushnell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Citrus County experienced over 9 hours of sub-freezing temperatures in some locations, with the lowest temperature of 25 degrees occurring at a mesonet station near Inverness. Temperaturs at this location were also at or below 27 degrees for 5 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay moved slowly north out of Caribbean Sea, then cut across south and central Florida into the Atlantic Ocean. Fay then slowly curved west and made landfall in the vicinity for Flagler Beach and Daytona Beach on Aug 21st. As the storm made landfall sustained winds along the coast were between 40 and 50 mph with gusts in the 60 to 65 mph range. Storm Surge ranged from 2 to 4 feet along the open coast and about 1 to 2 feet on the St Johns River.  Storm Surge Flooding occurred on the St Johns River on Thursday night August 21-22 in the Palatka area of Putnam County as northeasterly winds created a seiche effect driving river water southward.  On Friday morning August 22nd the winds became more southeasterly along the river and river water was driven north in the San Marco and Riverside areas of Downtown Jacksonville flooding roadways and some structures. River water depths of 4 to 6 inches flooded the Park Lane Condominium in Riverside. A Ford Bronco in the street outside of the condo floated in the river water a short distance into Memorial Park. Flash flooding occurred on McCoys Creek on Thursday night and Moncrief Creek on Friday. Both creeks are in Jacksonville, Duval County, Florida.  Several vehicles were stranded at Kings Road and McCoys Creek Blvd including one vehicle up to its roof. Areal flooding occurred over much of northeast Florida with roads and small creeks flooded. Minor flooding of structures occurred across the area. Numerous trees and power lines were downed with minor structural damage. High surf and beach erosion threatened five homes on the 3850 block of S Ponte Vedra Blvd in Vilano Beach, Florida. Several minor tornado touchdowns occurred in the area with no significant damage or injuries. Many north Florida creeks and Rivers experienced fresh water flooding with Black Creek, The St Marys River at McClenny, the St Johns River, south of Palatka, and the Sante Fe River experiencing the most significant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay moved slowly north out of Caribbean Sea, then cut across south and central Florida into the Atlantic Ocean. Fay then slowly curved west and made landfall in the vicinity for Flagler Beach and Daytona Beach on Aug 21st. As the storm made landfall sustained winds along the coast were between 40 and 50 mph with gusts in the 60 to 65 mph range. Storm Surge ranged from 2 to 4 feet along the open coast and about 1 to 2 feet on the St Johns River.  Storm Surge Flooding occurred on the St Johns River on Thursday night August 21-22 in the Palatka area of Putnam County as northeasterly winds created a seiche effect driving river water southward.  On Friday morning August 22nd the winds became more southeasterly along the river and river water was driven north in the San Marco and Riverside areas of Downtown Jacksonville flooding roadways and some structures. River water depths of 4 to 6 inches flooded the Park Lane Condominium in Riverside. A Ford Bronco in the street outside of the condo floated in the river water a short distance into Memorial Park. Flash flooding occurred on McCoys Creek on Thursday night and Moncrief Creek on Friday. Both creeks are in Jacksonville, Duval County, Florida.  Several vehicles were stranded at Kings Road and McCoys Creek Blvd including one vehicle up to its roof. Areal flooding occurred over much of northeast Florida with roads and small creeks flooded. Minor flooding of structures occurred across the area. Numerous trees and power lines were downed with minor structural damage. High surf and beach erosion threatened five homes on the 3850 block of S Ponte Vedra Blvd in Vilano Beach, Florida. Several minor tornado touchdowns occurred in the area with no significant damage or injuries. Many north Florida creeks and Rivers experienced fresh water flooding with Black Creek, The St Marys River at McClenny, the St Johns River, south of Palatka, and the Sante Fe River experiencing the most significant flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large outflow boundary from strong thunderstorms moved north across the Florida Straits the morning of October 22, 2008.  The east end of the boundary became nearly stationary across Key West, causing new thunderstorms to develop and train over the island.  Extensive street flooding occurred in Key West due to heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.70 inches was measured at Key West International Airport in less than two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain from Tropical Storm Fay produced widespread flooding across Brevard and central and southern Volusia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain from Tropical Storm Fay dropped as much as 15 inche of rain on the central and south parts of Brevard County. As many as 1600 homes were flooded. Damage was in excess of $70 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain from Tropical Storm Fay produced widespread flooding across Brevard and central and southern Volusia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As Tropical Storm Fay drifted north in the Volusia County off shore waters, hours of torrential rain fell across southern Volusia county. Close to 200 homes were flooded. Damage was near $13 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain bands from Tropical Storm Fay moving onshore from the Atlantic produced a tornado in southern Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado embedded in a rain band from Tropical Storm Fay moved onshore and struck the manufactured home community of Barefoot Bay. The tornado destroyed 9 mobile homes and damaged 59. The tornado blew the roof off and collapsed the exterior walls of the most severely damaged mobile home. Dozens of lightweight out buildings, awnings and car ports were damaged. Two minor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of August 19th, Tropical Storm Fay came onshore in southwest Florida, moving north-northeast toward Lake Okeechobee.  Fay remained well formed and actually intensified over land, exhibiting a classical tropical cyclone eye as it reached peak intensity over the western shore of Lake Okeechobee. Wind gusts of 58 mph were reported in the town of Okeechobee. By the early morning of August 20th, Tropical Storm Fay had moved to southern Brevard County, producing widespread wind gusts over 50 mph. Patrick AFB reported a gust to 62 mph. Fay produced torrential rain along the Space and Treasure coasts on the 20th as the circulation center moved up the Brevard County coast and into the near shore Atlantic waters near Edgewater.  Rainfall amounts on the 20th were near 8 to 9 inches in Martin, Saint Lucie and Indian River counties and from 5 to 16 inches in Brevard County.  On August 21st Fay drifted onshore in northern Volusia County and produced an additional 6 to 13 inches of rain in Brevard County and about 5 inches of rain in southern Volusia County. Orlando Executive Airport had a wind gust to 56 mph and Sanford reported a gust to 53 mph. A 35 year old woman drowned in the rough surf while swimming at Daytona Beach. On August 22nd, as Tropical Storm Fay moved across the Florida peninsula north of Ocala, an additional 7 to 10 inches of rain fell over Volusia County.  In Indian River County a 43 year old male motorist died after hydroplaning on a high water road and crashing his vehicle.  ||In Okeechobee County, about 70 homes were damaged by flood waters from heavy rain. A few homes received minor wind damage and 4300 homes were without power. Property damage was near $100 thousand.  Wind gusts of 58 mph were reported in the town of Okeechobee.  ||In Brevard County, the heaviest rain from Tropical Storm Fay fell, with widespread reports of 20 plus inches and a maximum of 27.65 inches in Melbourne. The rain flooded or damaged over 1,600 homes and businesses, producing a loss of near $60 million. An additional $10 million in damage to public infrastructure was reported. Three days of rough surf produced moderate beach erosion. An EF1 tornado tore through a large manufactured home community in Barefoot Bay. ||In Volusia County, Tropical Storm Fay produced widespread rainfall amounts of 10 inches with isolated reports of 20 inches. Over 200 homes were damaged by flood waters. Frequent wind gusts over 45 mph produced minor wind damage and power loss to 16,000 homes. Rough surf produced significant beach erosion at New Smyrna Beach where 50 feet of new sand was washed away. Estimated wind gusts were near 45 mph along the southeast coast. ||In Seminole County, as much as 15 inches of rain fell, with widespread coverage of 10 inches. About 500 homes were damaged by local rainfall flooding. Damage amounts were near $15 million. Wind gusts to 47 mph produced minor wind damage and power loss to 4,000 homes.   | |In St. Lucie County, rainfall amounts of 10 to 15 inches fell over most of the coastal region, flooding over 900 homes. Damage estimates were over $70 million. Wind gusts estimates were near 50 mph. A short lived EF0 tornado slightly damaged a building near Ft. Pierce. ||In Orange County, heavy rain of 10 to 13 inches fell, flooding only 5 homes. Wind gusts to 56 mph produced only minor wind damage. Total damage estimates were near $100 thousand.  ||In Martin County, rainfall amounts of 10 to 15 inches fell over most of the coastal region, flooding over 55 homes. Damage estimates were over $70 million. Wind gusts in the western part of the county near Lake Okeechobee were estimated to be near 50 mph. A short-lived EF0 tornado overturned a panel truck in Stuart. | |In Lake County, rainfall ranged from about 15 inches in Eustis to only 4 inches in the southwest part of the county. Only minor urban flooding was reported with the heavy rain.|  |In Indian River County, rainfall amounts of 15 to 22 inches fell over most of the coastal sections of the county. No flood damage was reported. Wind gust in the western part of the county near Lake Okeechobee were estimated to be near 50 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 1300 EST, a 35 year old woman was walking with her husband in waist-deep water at Daytona Beach during Tropical Storm Fay. They were hit by a huge wave and swept off their feet. The husband made it back to shore, but the wife was swept away and drowned. Ten minutes later, the woman's body was recovered about 75 feet north of where she was swept into the rough surf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area Tuesday night with strong high pressure behind the front bringing an arctic airmass over the state on strong northerly flow. Freezing temperatures settled into Levy county Wednesday morning and then settled across the entire area for Thursday morning, with many locations seeing freezing temperatures for six or more hours. North winds of 10 to 15 mph Thursday morning also allowed wind chill values to fall to 15 to 20 degrees across the area. Temperatures moderated for Friday morning, but freezing temperatures were still felt across the Nature Coast for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing conditions were felt across much of the county, with several locations reaching lows of 27 to 30 degrees. Strawberry farms mainly located in Plant City received damage to several acres as gusty winds prevented sprinklers from evenly coating the plants with ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure in place across the area allowed for moist low levels and light winds overnight. Patches of dense fog developed around 4AM and interacted with lingering smoke from a controlled burn near Polk City to produce about a 2 mile area of super-fog over Interstate 4 between US 27 and the Polk Parkway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A controlled burn from the previous afternoon was still producing smoke as patches of dense fog began to develop around 4AM. The smoke and fog interacted to produce super-fog in about a 2 mile area encompassing Interstate 4 near Polk City between US 27 and the Polk Parkway. The super-fog caused zero visibility for motorists and the result was a 70 vehicle pileup with 5 fatalities and 38 injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a mid and upper level trough moving east across South Florida and a developing warm frontal boundary provided the necessary atmospheric conditions for intense rains and flooding in the Boynton Beach area on January 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense rains affected Boynton Beach and the northwest section of Delray Beach during the late afternoon and evening hours of January 22nd. Maximum observed rainfall amounts were between 4 and 6 inches in Boynton Beach, although Doppler radar estimated as much as 10 inches of rain fell in just over 3 hours.||Numerous reports of flooding were reported. A trained spotter reported water getting into houses in the corridor west of Federal Highway and east of Congress Avenue between Boynton Beach Boulevard and Woolbright Road. Water rose to as high as 2 feet along sections of Congress Avenue. Significant flooding was reported at the parking lot of Boynton Beach mall. The I-95 on-ramp at Gateway Boulevard was closed due to the water depth, as was sections of Boynton Beach Boulevard. ||Dozens of vehicles stalled and 40 traffic accidents were reported due to the rain and standing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving line of thunderstorms produced 2 to 3 inches of rain that flooded secondary roads north of Interstate 10 in Escambia County.

Strong westerly winds ahead of a vigorous storm system, which moved northeast from the northern Gulf of Mexico into Alabama and Georgia, generated high surf, coastal flooding and beach erosion along the Bay and Gulf county coastline on the 10-11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Battering surf of six to eight feet along the Bay County coastline caused moderate beach erosion and coastal flooding.  At Mexico Beach,|three to four feet of sand depth was lost and the surf completely flooded the deck of a restaurant.  The beach lounge chairs were swept into the surf zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly winds ahead of a vigorous storm system, which moved northeast from the northern Gulf of Mexico into Alabama and Georgia, generated high surf, coastal flooding and beach erosion along the Bay and Gulf county coastline on the 10-11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large battering surf of eight to ten feet caused coastal flooding and beach erosion along the Gulf County coastline.  Moderate to major beach erosion occurred near County Road 30E at Cape San Blas.  The heavy surf broke over the jetties and rocks and across the only access road to St Joseph Peninsula.  A portion of U.S. Highway 98 just south of Port St Joe was completely under water during the peak of the coastal flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds associated with a line of heavy showers downed trees and power lines in Blountstown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed trees and power lines with widespread power outages were reported in Blountstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay emerged into the Apalachee Bay late Friday night and made its fourth landfall on Saturday, August 23rd near Carrabelle, Florida.  That morning, Fay had tapped into Gulf of Mexico moisture.  A large feeder band began affecting areas east of the Apalachicola River during the morning, followed by more intense bands, which trained over the same locations during the afternoon and evening hours, producing extremely high rainfall rates and subsequent flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads and low lying areas were flooded, including portions of U.S. Highway 19/27 between Cross City and Jonesboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay emerged into the Apalachee Bay late Friday night and made its fourth landfall on Saturday, August 23rd near Carrabelle, Florida.  That morning, Fay had tapped into Gulf of Mexico moisture.  A large feeder band began affecting areas east of the Apalachicola River during the morning, followed by more intense bands, which trained over the same locations during the afternoon and evening hours, producing extremely high rainfall rates and subsequent flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many roads and low lying areas were flooded.  More than sixteen inches of rain fell within a six hour period, with as much as twenty inches across central and northern portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced several reports of damaging winds and a tornado over portions of the Florida Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down just south of Interstate 10 at Mile Marker 168. An tractor-trailer was blown over on the interstate with only minor injuries to the driver.  The next area of damage was just east of the community of Hardaway, or three miles north of the interstate.  At this location numerous trees were snapped or uprooted with minor damage to two mobile homes.  A 19-foot boat was flipped over and shed were destroyed on Finnuff Road.  More significant damage was observed in the community of Mt Pleasant where a few homes were damaged.  A home on B.W. Martin Road sustained complete roof failure and a loss of a free standing carport.  The metal sheeting was scattered through the neighboring field and twisted like rope.  About a mile north of U.S. Highway 90 on Mt Pleasant Road, two homes sustained severe roof damage with large oak trees uprooted around the homes.  At this location, the tornado reached its peak intensity of EF1 with a path width of 150 yards prior to lifting as the parent supercell storm traveled northeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief, but extensive severe weather episode occurred across the Florida Big Bend during the morning of March 7, 2008. There were widespread reports of straight line wind damage across the area, with two confirmed tornadoes reported by NWS survey teams. There was also some urban street flooding in Tallahassee.  Nearly 10,000 Tallahassee customers were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several power poles down in the Southwood Plantation community near Apalachee Parkway and Idlewild Drive.  Several business signs were damaged along the Apalachee Parkway.  A large branch was hurtled through the roof of a business.  The awning of a restaurant was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southerly flow developed over north Florida and southeast Georgia as several convective complexes developed ahead of cold front and upper level trough over the Florida Panhandle. A southwesterly low level jet advected deep moisture off the Gulf of Mexico. This when combined with significant upper level divergence associated with right rear quadrant of the upper jet provided strong large scale lift to support severe convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees were blown down at the intersection of State Road 100 and County Road with one tree down on a power line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage reported to trees along State Road 24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief, but extensive severe weather episode occurred across the Florida Big Bend during the morning of March 7, 2008. There were widespread reports of straight line wind damage across the area, with two confirmed tornadoes reported by NWS survey teams. There was also some urban street flooding in Tallahassee.  Nearly 10,000 Tallahassee customers were without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay emerged into the Apalachee Bay late Friday night and made its fourth landfall on Saturday, August 23rd near Carrabelle, Florida.  That morning, Fay had tapped into Gulf of Mexico moisture.  A large feeder band began affecting areas east of the Apalachicola River during the morning, followed by more intense bands, which trained over the same locations during the afternoon and evening hours, producing extremely high rainfall rates and subsequent flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many roads and low lying areas were flooded.  Rainfall amounts ranged from six to ten inches across southern portions of the county, to as much as eighteen inches across northern portions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief, but extensive severe weather episode occurred across the Florida Big Bend during the morning of March 7, 2008. Nearly 10,000 Tallahassee customers were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout came ashore as a tornado at Keaton Beach.  Damage began along Keaton Beach Drive, just south of Beach Road, and continued east to Marina Drive where most of the significant damage occurred.  Sporadic tree and power line damage was observed near Marina Drive.  Fifteen homes between Marina Drive and Keaton Beach Drive sustained minor to moderate roof damage.  Several decks on these homes failed.  One home was destroyed when it was blown off its foundation and tossed into the road.  Two injuries were reported.  A few small boats were lofted into the air, with one boat moved over 100 feet.  The tornado quickly lifted over an open area east of Marina Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado moved north-northeast from just north of I-595 along SW 31st Ave to just south of Sunrise Blvd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down just north of Interstate 595 and moved across the Riverland Village, Melrose Park and Washington Park neighborhoods of west Fort Lauderdale. The tornado moved north-northeast, roughly paralleling 31st Avenue, before lifting just south of Sunrise Boulevard. The tornado's widest point was about one-eighth of a mile between SW 4th St and SW 8th St, or between Davie and Broward Boulevards. Minor damage to roofs was noted along with fences, awnings, power lines and trees blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very low astronomical tides were observed on Saturday, March 8, 2008 due to a combination of strong offshore winds and the new moon phase. At least 2 National Ocean Service tide stations had record low tide measurements.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fernandina Beach, Florida tide gauge measured -3.12 feet Mean Lower Low Water which was about 2.45 feet below predicted tide of -0.70 feet around 1942Z. Although not a record, this was roughly a foot from the record of -4.1 feet Mean Lower Low Water in 1940.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very low astronomical tides were observed on Saturday, March 8, 2008 due to a combination of strong offshore winds and the new moon phase. At least 2 National Ocean Service tide stations had record low tide measurements.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mayport, Florida tide gauge had measured low tide of -2.57 feet Mean Lower Low Water which was about 1.67 feet below predicted tide of -0.90 feet around 1930Z. Previous record was -2.28 feet Mean Lower Low Water on 2/18/1996.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area Tuesday night with strong high pressure behind the front bringing an arctic airmass over the state on strong northerly flow. Freezing temperatures settled into Levy county Wednesday morning and then settled across the entire area for Thursday morning, with many locations seeing freezing temperatures for six or more hours. North winds of 10 to 15 mph Thursday morning also allowed wind chill values to fall to 15 to 20 degrees across the area. Temperatures moderated for Friday morning, but freezing temperatures were still felt across the Nature Coast for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hard freeze occurred across much of the county, with the COOP station in Bushnell reaching a low of 24 degrees and the AWOS station in The Villages reaching 28 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong area of surface high pressure moved eastward from a position in western Tennessee with a strong north-northeast flow over the local coastal waters. In response a marine stratocumulus deck drifted over the Flagler County and southern St. Johns County areas. With the increase in low level moisture and cold 850 mb temperatures isolated flurries occurred mainly over southern St Johns and Flagler Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow flurries across Flagler County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line with severe weather and several tornadoes moved across north Florida and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service Storm Survey of the tornado track indicated most damage was EF1 scale with a small area of EF2 damage near NE Denver Street opposite a large field. Numerous Trees and power lines were snapped or blown over by the storm.  Truck and trailers were also blown over in an industrial park. Nineteen homes were destroyed, twenty-one suffered major damage, and twenty had minor damage.  Two businesses were destroyed with six suffering major damage.  One female fatality occurred when a tree went through her mobile home. A male indirect fatality occurred when trying to connect a power generator. The mesocyclone which spanned this tornado was tracked across Taylor, Lafayette, and Suwannee Counties prior to this touchdown and it later spanned tornadoes in Baker, Charlton and Nassau Counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong EF1 tornado first touched down west of Highway 29 and to the south of Molino Road. The tornado then moved northeast just to the south of Molino Road to near Old Molino and then crossed the Escambia River and dissipated just east of the river. The tornado damaged approximately 60 structures, including homes, storage buildings and businesses sustained varying degrees of damage ranging from minor to heavy. Forty of the structures were uninhabitable. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued into April across interior and western portions of South Florida, although beneficial rains early in the month were enough to remove the severe drought conditions (D2) from South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued into the first few days of April across interior and western sections of South Florida. However, heavy rains during the first week of April dropped from 2 to 5 inches of rain across the area. As a result, Glades, Hendry and Collier counties were dropped from the severe drought (D2) status. ||The rains also brought the level of Lake Okeechobee up to 10.5 feet. Nevertheless, water usage restrictions continued in effect throughout the month across interior and southwest Florida.||Total agricultural damage estimates from the severe drought which began in March 2007 are estimated to be near $1 billion in the state of Florida, with much of that in South Florida.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep southerly flow over South Florida and the coastal waters ahead of a frontal boundary across the northwestern sections of the Gulf of Mexico allowed strong to severe thunderstorms to move northward from Cuba across the straits and into much of South Florida.||A total of 25,000 customers lost power across South Florida as a result of the storms. 21,000 customers lost power in Miami-Dade and Broward counties, and 4800 in Palm Beach County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a wind gust to around 58 MPH with the passing severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary remained across South Florida for a second day as another short wave moved across the region during the afternoon hours allowing severe thunderstorms to develop over Palm Beach County. The storms produced wind damage and hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain across the Wellington area produced multiple reports of knee deep water in yards and across roadways.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout developed just offshore of Dekle Beach and came ashore as an EF0 tornado. The tornado, which lasted a few minutes, caused major roof damage to three homes.  Several other homes sustained minor damage to porches, screens and windows from wind-borne debris.  The damage path included Dekle Beach, Palmetto and Mexico Roads.  Power was knocked out at Dekle Beach.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across Glades, Hendry, and Collier counties and also across Lake Okeechobee producing a high wind gust at Rotenberger Wildlife Management Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall fell across the area on April 5.  The rains caused several roads to close due to high water.  Some of the inside lanes of U. S. Highway 90 near Pace had to be closed until the water had drained.  Rainfall totals of four to ten inches fell across the area.  Some of the rivers in the area also approached flood stage because of the heavy rains.  No major river flooding occurred because of the heavy rains.
Rip currents nearly claimed the life of a 67 year old male tourist.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 67 year old Canadian tourist was attempting to save a 10 year old boy who was trapped in a rip current at Lauderdale-By-The-Sea and he also became trapped in the strong current. The two people trapped in the rip current were then helped ashore by two others on boogie boards, but as the 4 people arrived on shore the tourist went into cardiac arrest, but was later revived at Holy Cross Hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three people became trapped in rip currents near 1900 South Ocean Drive on Hallandale Beach, and one person drowned.

The peak rainfall of 6.15 inches was recorded by a CoCoRahs volunteer 3.3 miles south southwest of Haines City.  Much of the rain fell between 2 PM EDT to 6 PM EDT.  Radar estimated precipitation showed an eleven mile wide band of 4 to 6 inches of rain from Plant City, across south Lakeland, and through Haines City.  Winter Haven's Gilbert Airport ASOS measured 5.18 inches of rain, observers at Lakeland Linder Airport measured 2.20 inches of rain, and the Peace River at Bartow recorded 3.58 inches for the day.

A large wildfire burned over the Everglades of western Miami-Dade County during the latter half of May. The fire known as the Mustang Corner Wildfire, got to within 3-5 miles of the far western edge of the Miami metropolitan area but did not damage any structures. The fire  prompted dense smoke advisories for the Miami Metropolitan area from May 17th to May 21st as dense smoke moved into the area during the night and early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mustang Corner Fire started during the morning of 14th and continued through the remainder of the month of May. The fire burned 39,465 acres in the Everglades National Park. The fire also prompted the evacuation of some 1,753 prisoners and 250 employees from the Everglades Correctional facility and 535 detainees from the Krome Detention Center as the fire closed within ten miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong east winds caused strong rip currents to form along the Atlantic beaches of southeast Florida. A swimmer nearly drowned at Carlin Park in Jupiter, and at least six people had to be rescued by Miami Beach Ocean Rescue lifeguards.
