EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small wildfire started during the afternoon of the 29th in Golden Gate Estates, quickly spreading and causing evacuations and engulfing homes and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire ignited in Golden Gate Estates and quickly spread up to 800 acres in just a few hours. The fire destroyed 3 homes, 10 out buildings, and 30 vehicles including boats and an RV. The fire also damaged an additional 7 homes, and produced power outages to about 4,000 customers. The fire was brought under control during the early evening of the 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of muck fires burned along the dry lakebed of southwestern Lake Okeechobee near the Glades and Hendry county shoreline for almost the entire month of May. Smoke from these fires periodically closed area roads and caused an accident involving fatalities in northern Hendry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wildfires burning muck or peat exposed by ongoing dry conditions along the southwestern shore of Lake Okeechobee spread to over 15 miles long from near Lakeport to near Clewiston and covered almost 30,000 acres as of May 31. Multiple road closures occurred nearly every night through the period, especially along US 27 and state roads 720 and 78, as the smoke settled near the ground and caused low visibilities. Several RVs were also threatened near the Moore Haven Marina & RV Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large grass fire ignited on a farm and burned equipment and 20 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire broke out near Southwest 227th Avenue and Southwest 232nd Street in the Redland area of western Miami-Dade County, covering about 20 acres and threatening a home before being extinguished. The fire consumed 20 acres of a 30 acre farm, two vehicles, and some farm equipment.

Sea breeze and outflow initiated storms produced heavy rainfall over localized areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters measured 2.75 inches of rainfall about 5 miles west of the airport in Gainesville around 5:30 pm EDT in one hour. At the same time, another spotter reported minor street flooding throughout Gainesville. By 6:30 pm EDT, a spotter measured 4.5 inches of rainfall in 2 hours with ditches and culverts full but not yet flooding. At 6:50 pm EDT, Alachua county central dispatch relayed reports of flooding in Gainesville, including in the Alachua General Hospital. Sandbags were used to retain water. A dumpster was observed floating in flood waters at an apartment complex. Several vehicles were stalled in flood waters with at least one with extensive flood damage to the interior. The cost of damage was estimated.

Around 8 pm EDT, flooding was reported at Mayfair Road and Beach Boulevard, and stalled cares were noted at Blanding Boulevard and 103rd Street. Around 912 pm EDT, a spotter measured 2.75 inches of rainfall in a two hour period in the Ortega area. At 923 pm EDT, a spotter reported 3 inches of rainfall and one foot of standing water at the corner of Chergboug Avenue South and Busac Drive about 4 miles southwest of Ortega.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front across Alabama and Georgia combined with high pressure over the Florida Keys to produce hot and breezy west winds across East Central Florida. On May 11th winds gusted from 30 to 40 mph and high temperatures were in the 90s. Wind gusts subsided to about 25 mph on May 12 and 15 mph on May 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly winds gusting to near 40 mph and high temperatures on May 11th began a four day fire event in Brevard County. The greatest damage occurred around and near the communities of Palm Bay and Malabar. Fire damage also occurred near the town of Cocoa. In all 162 homes were damaged or destroyed and over 10,000 acres burned. There were three minor injuries to fire fighters. Interstate 95 in Brevard County was shut down by smoke on numerous occasions. Damage was over 16 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An increasingly moist and unstable airmass combined with upper level energy in advance of a remnant MCS complex over Georgia spawned scattered severe storms across the forecast area during the afternoon and evening. Precipitable water values were 1.87 inches on the 12Z Jacksonville sounding with modified surface based CAPEs around 3500 J/kg and lifted indcies of -8 to -9 degrees Celcius.  In addition, 500 mb temperatures cooled to -9 degrees Celcius.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down in White Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mean 1000-500 mb layer trough was offshore of the Florida Atlantic coast. Drier, northerly flow limited convection over coastal southeast Georiga, but high precipitable water values lingered over central Florida with values around 2 inches. Modified sounding profiles indicated afternoon CAPEs of 3700 J/kg and enhanced the likelihood of strong winds in afternoon convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along State Road 231 east of Dukes and along State Road 121 and County Road 18.

The tree fell on the west side of County Road 309 in front of the Blessings Cafe and the power went out in Welaka at about 1 p.m. In San Mateo and East Palatka, a main feeder line and some secondary lines fell along with trees, cutting power to about 1,500 customers.

She then ran for cover while the roof lifted off. Destruction to the mobile home was 80% with 100% destruction occurring to an addition. Amount of damage to second home was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay affected South Florida on the 18th and 19th of August. The storm made landfall early in the morning of the 19th near Cape Romano, and moved over inland sections of South Florida during the morning and afternoon hours of the 19th, exiting the area near Lake Okeechobee. Fay caused tropical storm force winds, significant rainfall flooding in some areas, as well as two confirmed tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts ranged 7 to 10 inches over northern Collier, Hendry and Glades counties near the path of the center of Fay. Isolated areas in northeastern Hendry and southeastern Glades counties received well in excess of 10 inches, with Moore Haven recorded the highest storm total rainfall of 16.17 inches recorded in the 48-hour period ending at 8 AM on Wednesday, August 20th.  Extensive flooding was observed over the areas with the highest totals, especially over Hendry and Glades counties. Hardest hit areas were the Felda and Montura areas in Hendry County were maximum rainfall amounts ranged from around 10 inches in Felda to as much as 12 to 15 inches in the Montura area.

When the workers touched the crane they were knocked off their feet and disoriented. The workers were taken to Jackson Memorial Hospital to make sure they had no internal injuries.||It was believed that lightning had struck the crane minutes before the workers touched the metal surface and were shocked.

Water reached around 2 feet deep in some roadways and a few inches deep in some residences. Several roads were closed, including the main bridge connecting Marco Island to the mainland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions persisted through June over much of southwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions which started in April continued into much of June. Typical summer rainfall was slow to arrive to southwest Florida. As a result, the level of Lake Okeechobee reached a yearly low of 9.28 in mid-June before rising back up to almost 9.5 feet by the end of June as rains finally arrived.||Monthly observed rainfall totals varied widely from area to area, with a low of 3.81 at Raccoon Point in eastern Collier County to a high of 14.28 at Devils Garden in Hendry County. Naples received a total of 9.45 inches, most of this in the second half of June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief, but extensive severe weather episode occurred across the Florida Big Bend during the morning of March 7, 2008. There were widespread reports of straight line wind damage across the area, with two confirmed tornadoes reported by NWS survey teams. There was also some urban street flooding in Tallahassee.  Nearly 10,000 Tallahassee customers were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain in a short period of time caused urban flooding in Tallahassee.  Two feet of water was reported over Franklin Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Thursday, February 21, a warm front lifted northward across the northeast Gulf of Mexico and then stalled right along the coast.  A series of strong thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall tracked east-northeast across much of the Florida Panhandle and Big Bend.  Bay County was hit particularly hard with 7-10 inches of rain falling that day. This caused widespread flooding across the county, with many roads closed in Panama City.  A large area of 4-6 inch rainfall was observed during the evening and overnight hours across the Florida Big Bend, resulting in urban and small stream flooding.  In Tallahassee, a daily rainfall record which stood for 114 years was shattered by the 4.99-inch total recorded that day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Road 79 north of Vernon to Johnson Crossroad was closed by floodwaters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Thursday, February 21, a warm front lifted northward across the northeast Gulf of Mexico and then stalled right along the coast.  A series of strong thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall tracked east-northeast across much of the Florida Panhandle and Big Bend.  Bay County was hit particularly hard with 7-10 inches of rain falling that day. This caused widespread flooding across the county, with many roads closed in Panama City.  A large area of 4-6 inch rainfall was observed during the evening and overnight hours across the Florida Big Bend, resulting in urban and small stream flooding.  In Tallahassee, a daily rainfall record which stood for 114 years was shattered by the 4.99-inch total recorded that day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many paved roads throughout the central and southern part of the county were flooded and dirt roads were gutted from the torrential downpours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Chipola River overflowed its banks late on February 24, and crested a few feet above flood stage.  Areal flooding was concentrated just east of Marianna on Old U.S. Road and south of Marianna on Magnolia Road, near the river bank.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water rose around mobile homes located on Old U.S. Road just north of U.S. Highway 90.  At the lowest section of Old U.S. Road, closer to U.S. Highway 90, water nearly covered both lanes.  At the canoe rental business on Magnolia Road south of Marianna, river water covered the lower driveway, and the parking area at the Magnolia Road Bridge was flooded.  At the Citizens Lodge Park just north of Marianna, part of the property flooded, with some of the trails underwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tides along the Gulf Coast were 2 feet below normal in Fort Myers as Tropical Storm Fay made landfall.  Otherwise, tides were generally 1 to 3 feet above normal from Bonita Beach to Cedar Key as the winds became onshore.||The tide was 2.64 feet above normal at the Cedar Key C-MAN station as Fay moved west into the Gulf of Mexico north of the station.  This occurred at low tide so the effects were minimized.  The highest storm tide was just under 5 feet during high tide later in the afternoon and again around daybreak the next day. Minor flooding of a low lying road near the airport was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tide was 2.64 feet above normal at the Cedar Key C-MAN station as Fay moved west into the Gulf of Mexico north of the station.  This occurred at low tide so the effects were minimized.  The highest storm tide was just under 5 feet during high tide later in the afternoon and again around daybreak the next day. Minor flooding of a low lying road near the airport was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were down along a half mile portion of Highway 150 from the Cherry Lake intersection to NW Fire Fly Way.  A home on NW Fire Fly Way sustained minor damage to a large shed.  Several trees were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Thursday, February 21, a warm front lifted northward across the northeast Gulf of Mexico and then stalled right along the coast.  A series of strong thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall tracked east-northeast across much of the Florida Panhandle and Big Bend.  Bay County was hit particularly hard with 7-10 inches of rain falling that day. This caused widespread flooding across the county, with many roads closed in Panama City.  A large area of 4-6 inch rainfall was observed during the evening and overnight hours across the Florida Big Bend, resulting in urban and small stream flooding.  In Tallahassee, a daily rainfall record which stood for 114 years was shattered by the 4.99-inch total recorded that day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding began along Beach Drive, Thomas Drive, in the Cove and near the intersection of State Road 77 and 23rd Street, then spread until roads across the county were underwater.  By 8 pm CST, county officials reported severe flooding at State Road 79 and Pine Log Road, Joan Avenue and Front Beach Road, and County Road 2301 and Noel Road.  The county courthouse and Emergency Operations Center were closed due to severe flooding.  Twenty Parker residents were rescued from their apartments on Cherry Lane, where three feet of water had entered.  Several homes along 16th Street in Cedar Grove were surrounded by water.  Many streets in Springfield were underwater, and 30 to 40 homes in the Robindale subdivision took on water.  Schools were closed on February 22.  A 50-foot wide portion of John Pitts Road in northern Bay County washed away.  The flood gates at the west end of Deer Point Lake's dam were opened to release flood waters into North Bay near Southport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms on the 7th dropped penny to golfball hail in near Tallahassee and produced wind damage north of Madison.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported at the intersection of Woodville Highway and Capital Circle Southeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor beach erosion was observed along the beaches of St Joseph State Park on the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor beach erosion was observed along the beaches of St Joseph State park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms caused scattered reports of wind damage in Bay and Madison counties on the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were down in Panama City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms caused scattered reports of wind damage in Bay and Madison counties on the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were down throughout the city.  Some trees fell onto power lines with scattered outages reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms caused scattered reports of wind damage in Bay and Madison counties on the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds toppled numerous trees and power lines, with some across county roads.  Nearly 1,500 customeres were without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure ridging across the area allowed an afternoon sea breeze to develop. Scattered thunderstorms developed along the boundary and produced large hail and heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to quarter size hail was reported along Bee Ridge Road from Beneva Road east to Interstate 75.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure ridging across the area allowed an afternoon sea breeze to develop. Scattered thunderstorms developed along the boundary and produced large hail and heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a power pole on West Norvell Bryant Highway in Lecanto during heavy rain causing the pole to snap. The weight of the snapped pole and the saturated ground caused three other poles to lean.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay blanketed most of the state of Florida with over 6 inches of rain.  Only the Tampa Bay Area escaped Fay's deluge with rainfall generally less than one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer recorded 9.49 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure ridging across the area allowed an afternoon sea breeze to develop. Scattered thunderstorms developed along the boundary and produced large hail and heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured 2.95 inches of rain in one hour on Southern Manor Court causing minor street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure ridging across the area allowed an afternoon sea breeze to develop. Scattered thunderstorms developed along the boundary and produced large hail and heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters along 38th Avenue between 4th Street and 7th Street reported 4 inches of rain falling in one hour causing street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early evening thunderstorms on the 13th downed trees and power lines near Quincy and north of Panama City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were down south of State Road 388.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay blanketed most of the state of Florida with over 6 inches of rain.  Only the Tampa Bay Area escaped Fay's deluge with rainfall generally less than one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer reported 12.74 inches of rain. Numerous reports of 6-11 inches of rain were reported over the eastern two-thirds of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay blanketed most of the state of Florida with over 6 inches of rain.  Only the Tampa Bay Area escaped Fay's deluge with rainfall generally less than one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Southwest Florida Water Management District gauge recorded 7.27 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay blanketed most of the state of Florida with over 6 inches of rain.  Only the Tampa Bay Area escaped Fay's deluge with rainfall generally less than one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Southwest Florida Water Management District gauge recorded 7.10 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced winds in excess of 70 mph, which downed trees in Apalachicola and damaged several homes at Shell Point Village.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds were estimated at 70 mph near U.S. Highway 98 and Spring Creek Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two men drowned from rip currents during the Memorial holiday weekend.  There were more than 150 calls for water rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two men drowned from rip currents off Panama City Beach.  One of the victims died while trying to assist a distressed swimmer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms on the 29th caused scattered wind damage across portions of the Florida Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds caused minor damage to a home at the intersection of State Roads 81 and 181 in Prosperity.  Several trees and power lines were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late night thunderstorms brought hail, intense lightning, strong winds and several reports of funnel clouds across southern Bay County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than 2,500 Bay County residents lost power after a large tree fell on a power line on U.S. Highway 231 north of U.S. Higwhay 98.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from an afternoon thunderstorm destroyed a Sweetwater home in Liberty County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a large oak tree adjacent to a single-story wood frame home.  The fire spread from the tree into the home.  The home was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 39 year old male was struck by lightning in Munson.

Storm tides of three to five feet along the Panhandle coast associated with Hurricane Gustav produced flooding and minor beach erosion.  Several homes and roads in coastal Gulf, Wakulla and Franklin counties were affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five foot storm tides flooded several county roads and floors of local businesses in St Marks.  Homes were affected by flooding on Sunrise Lane, Blue Heron Way, Blue Crab Lane, Surf Road and Mashes Sands Road.  Fifteen homes sustained minor damage.  Flooding was also observed in Wooley Park and Shell Point.  State Road 363 at St Marks, Mashes Sands and Shell Point were closed.  Minor beach erosion was reported along the coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds on the 10th downed several trees near Sopchoppy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down near Sopchoppy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ike generated high surf which resulted in minor beach erosion and coastal flooding along the Panhandle coast from Walton to Franklin County on the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: St George Island State Park was closed due to high surf.  Apalachicola's Water Street experienced flooding, as well as in front of a local seafood retailer and behind the Franklin County Courthouse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storm tides of three to five feet along the Panhandle coast associated with Hurricane Gustav produced flooding and minor beach erosion.  Several homes and roads in coastal Gulf, Wakulla and Franklin counties were affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm tides of two to three feet were observed along the coast.  The high surf closed all beaches.  No beach erosion was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storm tides of three to five feet along the Panhandle coast associated with Hurricane Gustav produced flooding and minor beach erosion.  Several homes and roads in coastal Gulf, Wakulla and Franklin counties were affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm tides of three feet and minor beach erosion were reported along the coast.  Water rose up the sea wall, however no serious overwash was reported.  A small amount of water surrounded a hotel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storm tides of three to five feet along the Panhandle coast associated with Hurricane Gustav produced flooding and minor beach erosion.  Several homes and roads in coastal Gulf, Wakulla and Franklin counties were affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four foot storm tides resulted in flooding of Indian Pass Road.  A home on County Road 30A sustained major flood damage.  Cape San Blas Road (County Road 30E) was closed due to road damage.  Minor beach erosion was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storm tides of three to five feet along the Panhandle coast associated with Hurricane Gustav produced flooding and minor beach erosion.  Several homes and roads in coastal Gulf, Wakulla and Franklin counties were affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to five foot storm tides caused flooding along Alligator Point Road (County Road 370) with one lane of the road washed out at Alligator Point.  Bald Point Road was closed.  Four to six feet of water flooded back yards about a quarter mile up the Carrabelle River.  According to the EMA, the surge heights were similar to those observed with Hurricane Opal.  One road on St George Island sustained overwash in four places.  One sand dune was lost to the high surf, with minor beach erosion reported elsewhere.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning strikes a home in Southwest Ranches but causes no significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home just after 2 PM in Southwest Ranches. The strike ignited an air conditioning duct, but when the fire department arrived all they saw was smoke. The house was vented and no significant damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay affected South Florida on the 18th and 19th of August. The storm made landfall early in the morning of the 19th near Cape Romano, and moved over inland sections of South Florida during the morning and afternoon hours of the 19th, exiting the area near Lake Okeechobee. Fay caused tropical storm force winds, significant rainfall flooding in some areas, as well as two confirmed tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The center of Tropical Storm Fay moved across Key West early in the evening of the 18th and into the mainland of South Florida at Cape Romano shortly before 5 AM on the 19th. Minimum central pressure was 989 MB at landfall, but continued to decrease after landfall to 986 MB at Moore Haven on the southwest shore of Lake Okeechobee. Maximum sustained winds were estimated to be around 52 knots (60 MPH) at landfall, however as the storm tracked across the western Everglades and Southwest Florida the radar presentation continued to organize and winds increased to around 56 knots (65 MPH) around Moore Haven. A maximum wind gust of 69 knots (79 MPH) was recorded on a South Florida Water Management gauge on Lake Okeechobee as the storm passed. ||Wind gusts to tropical storm force were felt area-wide, with sustained tropical storm force winds experienced over portions of Mainland Monroe, Collier, Hendry and Glades counties as well as the immediate coastal sections of Miami-Dade, Broward and Palm Beach counties. Wind damage was most significant in the areas affected by tropical storm force sustained winds, primarily around Lake Okeechobee and interior sections of southwest Florida, with only minor wind damage elsewhere.||A maximum rainfall total of 16.17 inches was reported with this event at Moore Haven in Glades County. Flooding from these rains produced total damage estimates of $380,000, primarily in Glades and Hendry counties. Rainfall elsewhere ranged from 3-6 inches in southeast Florida, and 6-8 inches in southwest Florida, with isolated amounts up to 10 inches in coastal Palm Beach County. ||The height of the storm tide was around 5 feet in the Everglades City and Chokoloskee areas with total damage from the surge estimated at $60,000. Minimal storm surge was noted elsewhere. All the associated effects of Tropical Storm Fay in South Florida resulted in 1 fatality, 4 injuries, and $3.949 million in property damage. Two tornadoes produced $1.25 million in damage, but no injuries or fatalities were reported. The one fatality and 3 of the injuries were indirectly caused by Fay with a traffic accident in Palm Beach County. The direct injury occurred when a kite surfer on Fort Lauderdale Beach lost control during a squall and was slammed into a building along A1A.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay came ashore along the northeast Florida coast on the 21st.  Fay moved slowly westward across northern Florida, producing widespread heavy rainfall and strong winds. On the 22nd, Fay began to approach the waters of Apalachee Bay.  Fay emerged into Apalachee Bay late on the 22nd, and made its fourth Florida landfall on the 23rd near Carrabelle. By that morning, Fay had tapped into Gulf of Mexico moisture.  Intense rainbands developed and trained over the same locations in the Florida Big Bend for several hours on the 23rd producing rainfall totals exceeding 20 inches in some locations.  This resulted in record flooding on area rivers and flooding in Tallahassee and surrounding cities that has not been seen in many years.  By the 24th, Fay had weakened to a depression and continued moving westward into southeastern Mississippi.  Besides the torrential rainfall associated with Fay, sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph downed many trees and power lines.  Numerous homes were damaged by fallen trees as well as the tropical storm-force wind gusts.  A tornado briefly touced down in a wooded area of Jefferson County on the 23rd.  A Talquin Electric linesman was electrocuted while working to restore power in Quincy when a tree fell across a nearby power line.  A Jefferson County man lost control of his vehicle and crashed into a flooded wooded area and drowned.  A man was killed during a heavy rain storm in Bay County when he lost control of his vehicle and collided with a large pine tree.  A state of emergency was declared by the Governor for all but Walton County.  Individual assistance was granted for Gadsden, Jefferson, Leon, Liberty, Madison, Taylor and Wakulla counties.  Many county schools were closed on the 22nd and 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall from Fay varied between eight and 14 inches.  Several trees and power lines were down throughout the county.  A few dirt roads were washed out.  Two mobile homes were damaged by fallen trees.  Flooding was reported in Greenville.  About 120 people applied for state or federal assistance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outer spiral band associated with Hurricane Gustav produced a tornado which was sighted by motorist driving on Highway 41 in the Everglades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed in the Everglades near Tamiami Trail. A motorist driving along the highway observed a tornado near 40 mile bend.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds caused by the outer circulation of Hurricane Gustav produced rip currents along the Atlantic coast of South Florida. These rip currents produced multiple fatalities, one injury, and caused around 15 rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The outer edges of Hurricane Gustav produced easterly winds of 20 knots with higher gusts along the Atlantic coasts of Palm Beach, Broward, and Miami-Dade Counties, while Tropical Storm Hanna east of the area pushed swells toward the coast as well. This in turn created strong rip currents that drowned a North Dakota husband and wife couple at an unguarded beach in Lauderdale-by-the-Sea.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay affected South Florida on the 18th and 19th of August. The storm made landfall early in the morning of the 19th near Cape Romano, and moved over inland sections of South Florida during the morning and afternoon hours of the 19th, exiting the area near Lake Okeechobee. Fay caused tropical storm force winds, significant rainfall flooding in some areas, as well as two confirmed tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts ranged 7 to 10 inches over northern Collier, Hendry and Glades counties near the path of the center of Fay. Isolated areas in northeastern Hendry and southeastern Glades counties received well in excess of 10 inches, with Moore Haven recorded the highest storm total rainfall of 16.17 inches recorded in the 48-hour period ending at 8 AM on Wednesday, August 20th.  Extensive flooding was observed over the areas with the highest totals, especially over Hendry and Glades counties. Hardest hit areas were the Felda and Montura areas in Hendry County were maximum rainfall amounts ranged from around 10 inches in Felda to as much as 12 to 15 inches in the Montura area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay affected South Florida on the 18th and 19th of August. The storm made landfall early in the morning of the 19th near Cape Romano, and moved over inland sections of South Florida during the morning and afternoon hours of the 19th, exiting the area near Lake Okeechobee. Fay caused tropical storm force winds, significant rainfall flooding in some areas, as well as two confirmed tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The center of Tropical Storm Fay moved across Key West early in the evening of the 18th and into the mainland of South Florida at Cape Romano shortly before 5 AM on the 19th. Minimum central pressure was 989 MB at landfall, but continued to decrease after landfall to 986 MB. The maximum storm surge was around 3 feet. Maximum sustained winds were estimated to be around 52 knots (60 MPH) at landfall, however as the storm tracked across the western Everglades and Southwest Florida the radar presentation continued to organize and winds increased to around 56 knots (65 MPH) around Moore Haven. A maximum wind gust of 69 knots (79 MPH) was recorded on a South Florida Water Management gauge on Lake Okeechobee as the storm passed. In South Florida, a maximum rainfall total of 16.17 inches was reported with this event at Moore Haven in Glades County. The height of the storm tide was around 5 feet in Everglades City and Chokoloskee areas with minimal storm surge noted elsewhere. All the associated effects of Tropical Storm Fay in South Florida resulted in 1 fatality, 4 injuries, and $3.949M in property damage. Two tornadoes produced $1.25M in damage, but no injuries or fatalities were reported. The one fatality and 3 of the injuries were indirectly caused by Fay with a traffic accident in Palm Beach County. The direct injury occurred when a kite surfer on Fort Lauderdale Beach lost control during a squall and was slammed into a building along A1A.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay's wind speed diminished to a depression as the system moved through the western Florida panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay moved into Okaloosa county around 300 PM CST on August 23.  The storm moved through the panhandle during the afternoon and early evening bringing only Tropical depression conditions.  Fay's wind speed diminished to a depression during the afternoon and evening and the center moved into southwest Alabama around 1100 PM CST on August 23.  The western Florida panhandle was put under a Tropical Storm watch at 900 AM CST on August 22.  The watch was upgraded to a Tropical Storm Warning at 900 PM CST on August 22.  All tropical watches and warnings were dropped at 900 PM CST on August 23. Storm total rainfall across the western Florida panhandle was generally less than an inch.  The highest amounts were in Santa Rosa county with both Milton and Jay recording around 3 inches.  Sustained winds were less than 25 knots (28 mph) with Milton recording the highest with 23 knots (26 mph).  The peak wind speed was 41 knots (47 mph) at Milton.  The lowest sea level pressure was 998 mb at Milton.  Coastal storm tides of 1 foot or less above astronomical tide levels were observed, with only minor beach erosion reported.  No injuries or deaths occurred.  No property damage was reported.

The storm made landfall early in the morning of the 19th near Cape Romano, and moved over inland sections of South Florida during the morning and afternoon hours of the 19th, exiting the area near Lake Okeechobee. Fay caused tropical storm force winds, significant rainfall flooding in some areas, as well as two confirmed tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado moved through a number of Equine Farms and Polo Grounds as well as two subdivisions in Wellington. Worst damage was to Palm Beach Equine Clinic, 13125 Southfield Road, where roofs were blown off of stables, power poles snapped, and many trees down in criss-crossing patterns. The Equine Veterinary roof was more than 95 percent detiled, a heavy trailer was tossed about 40 yards from its previous location northwest of the International Polo Club, and an apartment home roof near Folkstone Circle was about 70 percent detiled. Maximum estimated wind speed was around 115 MPH. Most of the track was around 70 to 80 yards wide, but at the widest point the tornado was around 100 yards in width.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening upper level trough over the southeast region lifted a deep plume of tropical moisture over the forecast area. A strong low level 850 mb jet of 50-55 knots was in place. Backing winds advected instability inland from the Gulf of Mexico across north Florida which coincided with the passing of a second pre-frontal squall line as it tracked across north Florida and Southeast Georgia in the afternoon. Low level storm relative helicities increased to 200-350 M2/S2. Widespread severe storms developed across the forecast area during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported trees blown down between Jasper and Jennings. Most of the damage was near Highway 41 North and State Road 6. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay made its first of four Florida landfalls at Key West shortly before 5 PM on Monday, August 18th. Maximum sustained winds were 60 mph and the minimum pressure was 998 MB.  Fay made its first peninsular landfall at Cape Romano shortly before 5 AM on Tuesday, August 19th with maximum sustained winds of 60 mph and lowest pressure of 989 MB. The center of the storm moved over the southeastern corner of Highlands County then northeast into the Atlantic where it stalled on the 20th offshore of Daytona Beach.  Fay began to move slowly northwest on the 21st with the center reaching the Levy/Gilchrist county line late Friday morning on August 22nd.  Fay moved into the Gulf of Mexico late in the evening then made a fourth Florida landfall early Saturday morning the 23rd in the Pan Handle.||Tropical Storm Fay caused $1.44 million in damage to west central and southwest Florida. Flooding caused $970,000 to roads and homes, while wind caused $470,000 in mostly minor damage to numerous homes, especially in Lee County.  The flood and tornado monetary damage were listed in their respective Storm Data categories. ||Lee County���Heavy rains of 6-14 inches caused extensive sheet flow flooding from the Imperial River that runs through Bonita Springs. Flood damage was reported to numerous roads and homes with 500 residents evacuated to shelters.  The preliminary damage estimates included $250,000 of flood damage to roads and $500,000 of flood and wind damage to homes with 170 manufactured homes condemned. Many of these homes had unpermitted modifications to increase their size.  These modifications were destroyed leaving gaping holes in the home. Minor beach erosion was reported.  The tide was 2 feet below normal as Fay made landfall then was 1 to 2 feet above normal for two days due to onshore winds. ||Charlotte County���The Punta Gorda Airport recorded a peak wind of 47 MPH at 10:13 AM EDT on the 19th.  Minor beach erosion occurred but no other significant damage was reported. ||Highlands County���A 54-year-old male died on August 16th from carbon monoxide poisoning as he tested his backup generator in his garage in Lake Placid. This was considered an indirect fatality due to Fay.  Widespread heavy rains of 5-12 inches caused $150,000 in flood damage to washed out roads and culverts.  Prefabrication metal buildings sustained wind damage that allowed rain into the buildings of three fire stations and several buildings of an electric coop with water damage estimated at $175,000.  Five homes along Arbuckle Creek received flood damage totaling $70,000.  ||DeSoto County���Radar estimated precipitation showed widespread 6-10 inches in the eastern part of the county and 3-6 inches in the western part.  A peak wind gust around 45 MPH was recorded at the Emergency Operations Center in Arcadia after Fay had passed to the east.  No significant wind or flooding damage was reported in the county. ||Hardee County���A wind gust of 42 MPH was recorded at the Emergency Operations Center in Wauchula on the backside of Fay.  A doublewide mobile home in Zolfo Springs lost half of its roof and received water damage.  ||Polk County���Widespread six to eight inches of rain fell in the eastern third of the county.  Radar estimated rainfall of 14 inches caused overland flooding just south of the intersection of Highway 60 and the Kissimmee River. Seven homes received up to 3 feet of water that caused $250,000 in damage.  ||Sumter County���Widespread heavy rain of 6-8 inches was reported along the north county line. The peak wind gust at the airport at The Villages was 46 MPH at on the 22nd at 4:45 PM EDT.  No significant flood or wind damage was reported.  Sporadic tree damage brought down several power lines and poles with an estimated damage of $20,000.  ||Hernando County���A peak wind gust of 47 MPH was recorded at the Homosassa COMPS station at 8:30 AM and a  peak wind gust of 40 MPH was recorded at the Brooksville Airport at 9 AM; both on the 22nd.   Rainfall reports and radar estimated rainfall ranged between 4-6 inches across the county.  The rain caused sink holes in the Spring Hill area and shallow flooding on Pine Island.  Downed trees took down power lines in the Brooksville and Ridge Manor areas.  A tree fell on a home in Brooksville and an older mobile home lost part of its roof.||Citrus County���Widespread heavy rain of 6-9 inches fell across the county.  Sporadic tree damage was found throughout the county but no major damage was reported.  Minor flooding due to above normal tides was reported in Old Homosassa and Crystal River.  ||Levy County���Tides were 2 to 2.5 feet above normal Friday afternoon and evening of August 22nd.  The total storm tide reached 5 feet.  A peak wind gust of 49 MPH was recorded at the Cedar Key C-MAN station on the 22nd at 11:09 AM EDT.  Numerous trees were downed in the west part of Morriston and caused damage to a home and a van.  Widespread heavy rain fall of 6-12 inches soaked the ground. The combination of the wind and wet ground knocked down num||Pasco, Pinellas, Hillsborough, Manatee, and Sarasota Counties���Minor beach erosion was reported with tides about a foot above normal. Several wind gusts around tropical storm strength were recorded along the beaches but were limited in duration.  The surf along the Gulf beaches were 3-4 feet and numerous rip currents were reported.

Tropical Storm Fay's wind speed diminished to a depression as the system moved through the western Florida panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay moved into Okaloosa county around 300 PM CST on August 23.  The storm moved through the panhandle during the afternoon and early evening bringing only Tropical depression conditions.  Fay's wind speed diminished to a depression during the afternoon and evening and the center moved into southwest Alabama around 1100 PM CST on August 23.  The western Florida panhandle was put under a Tropical Storm watch at 900 AM CST on August 22.  The watch was upgraded to a Tropical Storm Warning at 900 PM CST on August 22.  All tropical watches and warnings were dropped at 900 PM CST on August 23. Storm total rainfall across the western Florida panhandle was generally less than an inch.  The highest amounts were in Santa Rosa county with both Milton and Jay recording around 3 inches.  Sustained winds were less than 25 knots (28 mph) with Milton recording the highest with 23 knots (26 mph).  The peak wind speed was 41 knots (47 mph) at Milton.  The lowest sea level pressure was 998 mb at Milton.  Coastal storm tides of 1 foot or less above astronomical tide levels were observed, with only minor beach erosion reported.  No injuries or deaths occurred.  No property damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds caused by the outer circulation of Hurricane Gustav produced rip currents along the Atlantic coast of South Florida. These rip currents produced multiple fatalities, one injury, and caused around 15 rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The outer edges of Hurricane Gustav produced easterly winds of 20 knots with higher gusts along the Atlantic coast Palm Beach, Broward, and Miami-Dade Counties while Tropical Storm Hanna pushed swells toward the coast as well. This in turn created strong rip currents that drowned a 14 year old boy at John Lloyd State Park in Hollywood. The boy went missing at around 6:30 while swimming with his friend, and was recovered by the U.S. Coast Guard the next morning. The friend was rescued by Ocean Rescue officials and taken to a local hospital. The 12 year old boy was in stable condition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay made its first of four Florida landfalls at Key West shortly before 5 PM on Monday, August 18th. Maximum sustained winds were 60 mph and the minimum pressure was 998 MB.  Fay made its first peninsular landfall at Cape Romano shortly before 5 AM on Tuesday, August 19th with maximum sustained winds of 60 mph and lowest pressure of 989 MB. The center of the storm moved over the southeastern corner of Highlands County then northeast into the Atlantic where it stalled on the 20th offshore of Daytona Beach.  Fay began to move slowly northwest on the 21st with the center reaching the Levy/Gilchrist county line late Friday morning on August 22nd. Many of these homes had unpermitted modifications to increase their size.  These modifications were destroyed leaving gaping holes in the home. Minor beach erosion was reported. Strong northeast flow around a 1035 mb surface high centered over New England and a developing Gulf of Mexico storm system brought strong winds and rough surf to the north Florida coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flagler Beach Fire Department reported 6 to 8 foot surf at Flagler Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening upper level trough over the southeast region lifted a deep plume of tropical moisture over the forecast area. A strong low level 850 mb jet of 50-55 knots was in place. Backing winds advected instability inland from the Gulf of Mexico across north Florida which coincided with the passing of a second pre-frontal squall line as it tracked across north Florida and Southeast Georgia in the afternoon. Low level storm relative helicities increased to 200-350 M2/S2. Widespread severe storms developed across the forecast area during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along State Road six and near the Agricultural Station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay affected South Florida on the 18th and 19th of August. The storm made landfall early in the morning of the 19th near Cape Romano, and moved over inland sections of South Florida during the morning and afternoon hours of the 19th, exiting the area near Lake Okeechobee. Fay caused tropical storm force winds, significant rainfall flooding in some areas, as well as two confirmed tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to Fay remaining at tropical storm strength and the rather limited nature of its wind field, storm surge and coastal flooding impacts were rather minor. The highest storm tide was estimated to be in the Everglades City/Chokoloskee areas, where the maximum storm tide was in the 5 foot range according to tide gauge data and estimates from local officials. Water of 1-2 feet deep was observed in Everglades City, and the Chokoloskee Causeway was under water for a short time on the morning of the 19th. Minimal storm surge was noted elsewhere, although moderate to locally severe beach erosion occurred in Naples.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay moved slowly north out of Caribbean Sea, then cut across south and central Florida into the Atlantic Ocean. Fay then slowly curved west and made landfall in the vicinity for Flagler Beach and Daytona Beach on Aug 21st. As the storm made landfall sustained winds along the coast were between 40 and 50 mph with gusts in the 60 to 65 mph range. Storm Surge ranged from 2 to 4 feet along the open coast and about 1 to 2 feet on the St Johns River.  Storm Surge Flooding occurred on the St Johns River on Thursday night August 21-22 in the Palatka area of Putnam County as northeasterly winds created a seiche effect driving river water southward.  On Friday morning August 22nd the winds became more southeasterly along the river and river water was driven north in the San Marco and Riverside areas of Downtown Jacksonville flooding roadways and some structures. River water depths of 4 to 6 inches flooded the Park Lane Condominium in Riverside. A Ford Bronco in the street outside of the condo floated in the river water a short distance into Memorial Park. Flash flooding occurred on McCoys Creek on Thursday night and Moncrief Creek on Friday. Both creeks are in Jacksonville, Duval County, Florida.  Several vehicles were stranded at Kings Road and McCoys Creek Blvd including one vehicle up to its roof. Areal flooding occurred over much of northeast Florida with roads and small creeks flooded. Minor flooding of structures occurred across the area. Numerous trees and power lines were downed with minor structural damage. High surf and beach erosion threatened five homes on the 3850 block of S Ponte Vedra Blvd in Vilano Beach, Florida. Several minor tornado touchdowns occurred in the area with no significant damage or injuries. Many north Florida creeks and Rivers experienced fresh water flooding with Black Creek, The St Marys River at McClenny, the St Johns River, south of Palatka, and the Sante Fe River experiencing the most significant flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay affected South Florida on the 18th and 19th of August. The storm made landfall early in the morning of the 19th near Cape Romano, and moved over inland sections of South Florida during the morning and afternoon hours of the 19th, exiting the area near Lake Okeechobee. Fay caused tropical storm force winds, significant rainfall flooding in some areas, as well as two confirmed tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The center of Tropical Storm Fay moved across Key West early in the evening of the 18th and into the mainland of South Florida at Cape Romano shortly before 5 AM on the 19th. Minimum central pressure was 989 MB at landfall, but continued to decrease after landfall to 986 MB at Moore Haven on the southwest shore of Lake Okeechobee. Maximum sustained winds were estimated to be around 52 knots (60 MPH) at landfall, however as the storm tracked across the western Everglades and Southwest Florida the radar presentation continued to organize and winds increased to around 56 knots (65 MPH) around Moore Haven. A maximum wind gust of 69 knots (79 MPH) was recorded on a South Florida Water Management gauge on Lake Okeechobee as the storm passed. ||Wind gusts to tropical storm force were felt area-wide, with sustained tropical storm force winds experienced over portions of Mainland Monroe, Collier, Hendry and Glades counties as well as the immediate coastal sections of Miami-Dade, Broward and Palm Beach counties. Wind damage was most significant in the areas affected by tropical storm force sustained winds, primarily around Lake Okeechobee and interior sections of southwest Florida, with only minor wind damage elsewhere.||A maximum rainfall total of 16.17 inches was reported with this event at Moore Haven in Glades County. Flooding from these rains produced total damage estimates of $380,000, primarily in Glades and Hendry counties. Rainfall elsewhere ranged from 3-6 inches in southeast Florida, and 6-8 inches in southwest Florida, with isolated amounts up to 10 inches in coastal Palm Beach County. ||The height of the storm tide was around 5 feet in the Everglades City and Chokoloskee areas with total damage from the surge estimated at $60,000. Minimal storm surge was noted elsewhere. All the associated effects of Tropical Storm Fay in South Florida resulted in 1 fatality, 4 injuries, and $3.949 million in property damage. Two tornadoes produced $1.25 million in damage, but no injuries or fatalities were reported. The one fatality and 3 of the injuries were indirectly caused by Fay with a traffic accident in Palm Beach County. The direct injury occurred when a kite surfer on Fort Lauderdale Beach lost control during a squall and was slammed into a building along A1A.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay came ashore along the northeast Florida coast on the 21st.  Fay moved slowly westward across northern Florida, producing widespread heavy rainfall and strong winds. On the 22nd, Fay began to approach the waters of Apalachee Bay.  Fay emerged into Apalachee Bay late on the 22nd, and made its fourth Florida landfall on the 23rd near Carrabelle. By that morning, Fay had tapped into Gulf of Mexico moisture.  Intense rainbands developed and trained over the same locations in the Florida Big Bend for several hours on the 23rd producing rainfall totals exceeding 20 inches in some locations.  This resulted in record flooding on area rivers and flooding in Tallahassee and surrounding cities that has not been seen in many years.  By the 24th, Fay had weakened to a depression and continued moving westward into southeastern Mississippi.  Besides the torrential rainfall associated with Fay, sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph downed many trees and power lines.  Numerous homes were damaged by fallen trees as well as the tropical storm-force wind gusts.  A tornado briefly touced down in a wooded area of Jefferson County on the 23rd.  A Talquin Electric linesman was electrocuted while working to restore power in Quincy when a tree fell across a nearby power line.  A Jefferson County man lost control of his vehicle and crashed into a flooded wooded area and drowned.  A man was killed during a heavy rain storm in Bay County when he lost control of his vehicle and collided with a large pine tree.  A state of emergency was declared by the Governor for all but Walton County.  Individual assistance was granted for Gadsden, Jefferson, Leon, Liberty, Madison, Taylor and Wakulla counties.  Many county schools were closed on the 22nd and 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall from Fay varied between 12 and 18 inches.  Several trees and power lines were down throughout the county.  Fifteen homes were damaged by fallen trees and flooding.  A few county roads were closed and some dirt roads were washed out.  The storm tide rose to three feet, which is the top of the sea wall at Keaton Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm occurred across the Big Cypress National Preserve and interstate 75.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed across the Big Cypress National Preserve and moved northwest across interstate 75 producing hail the size of quarters for around ten minutes at the Collier County rest area on Alligator Alley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved across the cities of Coral Springs and Plantation causing minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved across portions of metropolitan Broward County causing damage in Coral Springs by tipping a gas station sign and a lamp post.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Florida Highway Patrol trooper was struck by lightning while investigating a crash on interstate 95.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 5:30 PM a trooper from the Florida Highway Patrol was investigating a crash on interstate 95 in Palm Beach County when he was struck by lightning. The trooper was able to radio for help and was taken to Bethesda Memorial Hospital along with two other people who were involved in the crash. All three people injured by the strike were later released by the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed along the inland-pushing sea breeze over the city of Hialeah. Favorable wind profiles along the sea breeze front helped to produce a brief tornado that damaged 8 structures. Other storms which formed along the sea breeze front in Palm Beach County caused  lightning damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced an EF1 Tornado with the highest estimated wind speeds near 90 MPH. The tornado began at West 22nd Street just west of West 1st Avenue where vehicles were moved and flipped at a tow yard. Roof damage occurred to at least five separate apartment complexes, with the worst damage between 22nd and 24th streets, but 8 structures were damaged overall. The tornado lifted at West 28th Street between Palm Avenue and West 1 Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over the southeast sections of Broward County producing a high wind gust and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over southeast sections of Broward County and passed over the North Perry Airport in the Pembroke Pines/Hollywood area. The storm produced a wind gust of 71 MPH along with small hail half an inch in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Nassau County Emergency Management reported a levee failure on Hampton Lake in northwest Nassau county, located near U.S. Highway 1 at the Nassau and Charlton county line.  The levee had been compromised during recent tropical storms, and the owners neglected to fix the problem.  The Nassau County Sheriffs office was evacuating some homeowners downstream of the lake and closed Lake Hampton road between U.S. Highway 1 and Kings Ferry Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake Hampton Road was closed to flood water steadily leaving the lake. Most of the water that was leaving the lake was flowing under Lake Hampton Road  through culverts as the lake eventually drained into the St. Marys River. Several trees had been washed into the drainage basin area between the levee and culverts and the debris was being monitored to ensure that it did not clog the culverts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay came ashore along the northeast Florida coast on the 21st.  Fay moved slowly westward across northern Florida, producing widespread heavy rainfall and strong winds. On the 22nd, Fay began to approach the waters of Apalachee Bay.  Fay emerged into Apalachee Bay late on the 22nd, and made its fourth Florida landfall on the 23rd near Carrabelle. By that morning, Fay had tapped into Gulf of Mexico moisture.  Intense rainbands developed and trained over the same locations in the Florida Big Bend for several hours on the 23rd producing rainfall totals exceeding 20 inches in some locations.  This resulted in record flooding on area rivers and flooding in Tallahassee and surrounding cities that has not been seen in many years.  By the 24th, Fay had weakened to a depression and continued moving westward into southeastern Mississippi.  Besides the torrential rainfall associated with Fay, sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph downed many trees and power lines.  Numerous homes were damaged by fallen trees as well as the tropical storm-force wind gusts.  A tornado briefly touced down in a wooded area of Jefferson County on the 23rd.  A Talquin Electric linesman was electrocuted while working to restore power in Quincy when a tree fell across a nearby power line.  A Jefferson County man lost control of his vehicle and crashed into a flooded wooded area and drowned.  A man was killed during a heavy rain storm in Bay County when he lost control of his vehicle and collided with a large pine tree.  A state of emergency was declared by the Governor for all but Walton County.  Individual assistance was granted for Gadsden, Jefferson, Leon, Liberty, Madison, Taylor and Wakulla counties.  Many county schools were closed on the 22nd and 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall from Fay varied between one and three inches.  There were several downed trees and power lines throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay moved slowly north out of Caribbean Sea, then cut across south and central Florida into the Atlantic Ocean. Fay then slowly curved west and made landfall in the vicinity for Flagler Beach and Daytona Beach on Aug 21st. As the storm made landfall sustained winds along the coast were between 40 and 50 mph with gusts in the 60 to 65 mph range. Storm Surge ranged from 2 to 4 feet along the open coast and about 1 to 2 feet on the St Johns River.  Storm Surge Flooding occurred on the St Johns River on Thursday night August 21-22 in the Palatka area of Putnam County as northeasterly winds created a seiche effect driving river water southward.  On Friday morning August 22nd the winds became more southeasterly along the river and river water was driven north in the San Marco and Riverside areas of Downtown Jacksonville flooding roadways and some structures. River water depths of 4 to 6 inches flooded the Park Lane Condominium in Riverside. A Ford Bronco in the street outside of the condo floated in the river water a short distance into Memorial Park. Flash flooding occurred on McCoys Creek on Thursday night and Moncrief Creek on Friday. Both creeks are in Jacksonville, Duval County, Florida.  Several vehicles were stranded at Kings Road and McCoys Creek Blvd including one vehicle up to its roof. Areal flooding occurred over much of northeast Florida with roads and small creeks flooded. Minor flooding of structures occurred across the area. Numerous trees and power lines were downed with minor structural damage. High surf and beach erosion threatened five homes on the 3850 block of S Ponte Vedra Blvd in Vilano Beach, Florida. Several minor tornado touchdowns occurred in the area with no significant damage or injuries. Many north Florida creeks and Rivers experienced fresh water flooding with Black Creek, The St Marys River at McClenny, the St Johns River, south of Palatka, and the Sante Fe River experiencing the most significant flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 19th Tropical Storm Fay came on shore in south west Florida moving north northeast toward Lake Okeechobee. By the early morning of August 20th Tropical Storm Fay had moved to southern Brevard County producing rainfall amounts ranging from 10 to 15 inches in Martin, Saint Lucie, Okeechobee, and Indian River counties. Okeechobee reported 69 homes damaged by flood waters and a total of $1.2 million. Indian River and St. Lucie counties reported a combined loss in citrus of $10 million. One indirect fatality occurred in Indian River County when a car hydroplaned and crashed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Gustav moved west-northwest through the northwest Caribbean Sea, crossing over western Cuba before continuing into the Gulf of Mexico. Tropical storm force winds spread over the Middle and Lower Florida Keys of Monroe County August 30 through 31, 2008.  Storm total rainfall was very light, owing to the large distance between the Florida Keys and the core of the hurricane. There was minor tidal overwash from the Atlantic side of the Keys due to a minor storm surge and large seas in the Florida Straits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Gustav passed well southwest of the Florida Keys of Monroe County during the night from August 30 to 31, 2008. Tropical storm force winds affected the Middle and Lower Florida Keys, with the highest sustained wind in the Middle Florida Keys measured at 31 knots (36 mph) at Key West International Airport, with gusts to 44 knots (51 mph).  Storm total rainfall was highest at Marathon, where 2.61 inches was recorded. The lowest sea level pressure was reported to be 1004.1 mb at Key West International Airport, and 1001.2 mb at sea at Pulaski Shoal Light, located about 7 miles east-northeast of Dry Tortugas National Park.  Coastal storm tides peaked at 2.1 feet above sea level at Key West Harbor, but were estimated at 2.5 feet above sea level along the south shore of Key West.  Storm tides peaked at 1.5 feet above sea level along the south shores of the Middle Keys, which was 1.0 foot above astronomical tides. Property damage is unknown, due to blending of Monroe County damage assessments with Hurricane Ike, which affected the Keys less than two weeks later.  One direct fatality occurred at sea 20 miles south of Dry Tortugas... approximately 80 miles west southwest of Key West.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay came ashore along the northeast Florida coast on the 21st.  Fay moved slowly westward across northern Florida, producing widespread heavy rainfall and strong winds. On the 22nd, Fay began to approach the waters of Apalachee Bay.  Fay emerged into Apalachee Bay late on the 22nd, and made its fourth Florida landfall on the 23rd near Carrabelle. By that morning, Fay had tapped into Gulf of Mexico moisture.  Intense rainbands developed and trained over the same locations in the Florida Big Bend for several hours on the 23rd producing rainfall totals exceeding 20 inches in some locations.  This resulted in record flooding on area rivers and flooding in Tallahassee and surrounding cities that has not been seen in many years.  By the 24th, Fay had weakened to a depression and continued moving westward into southeastern Mississippi.  Besides the torrential rainfall associated with Fay, sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph downed many trees and power lines.  Numerous homes were damaged by fallen trees as well as the tropical storm-force wind gusts.  A tornado briefly touced down in a wooded area of Jefferson County on the 23rd.  A Talquin Electric linesman was electrocuted while working to restore power in Quincy when a tree fell across a nearby power line.  A Jefferson County man lost control of his vehicle and crashed into a flooded wooded area and drowned.  A man was killed during a heavy rain storm in Bay County when he lost control of his vehicle and collided with a large pine tree.  A state of emergency was declared by the Governor for all but Walton County.  Individual assistance was granted for Gadsden, Jefferson, Leon, Liberty, Madison, Taylor and Wakulla counties.  Many county schools were closed on the 22nd and 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall from Fay varied between one and three inches.  There were several downed trees and power lines throughout the county.  A dirt roads were washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening upper level trough over the southeast region lifted a deep plume of tropical moisture over the forecast area. A strong low level 850 mb jet of 50-55 knots was in place. Backing winds advected instability inland from the Gulf of Mexico across north Florida which coincided with the passing of a second pre-frontal squall line as it tracked across north Florida and Southeast Georgia in the afternoon. Low level storm relative helicities increased to 200-350 M2/S2. Widespread severe storms developed across the forecast area during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 55 to 60 mph were rocking a spotters car at 1100 11th Street SW in Live Oak.

Flooding along the St Marks River set a record with a stage of 13.88 feet, or 2.1 feet above the previous record stage.  The Ochlockonee River south of the C.H. Corn Hydro dam rose to flood stage in the western part of the county.  Storm tides of four feet on the morning of the 24th at St Marks caused flooding of several buildings in St Marks, with four to six inches of water near the time of high tide.  Twenty-nine homes were damaged and two were destroyed by fallen trees and flooding.  Seven homes were inaccessible due to high water.

A squall line was moving across the area when the collapsed occurred. The KJAX Doppler radar indicated 45 to 50 knot velocities at approximately 600 feet msl. Each crane cost $6 million.

Backing winds advected instability inland from the Gulf of Mexico across north Florida which coincided with the passing of a second pre-frontal squall line as it tracked across north Florida and Southeast Georgia in the afternoon. Low level storm relative helicities increased to 200-350 M2/S2. Widespread severe storms developed across the forecast area during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous power lines were blown down across Eastern Duval county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay moved slowly north out of Caribbean Sea, then cut across south and central Florida into the Atlantic Ocean. Fay then slowly curved west and made landfall in the vicinity for Flagler Beach and Daytona Beach on Aug 21st. As the storm made landfall sustained winds along the coast were between 40 and 50 mph with gusts in the 60 to 65 mph range. Storm Surge ranged from 2 to 4 feet along the open coast and about 1 to 2 feet on the St Johns River.  Storm Surge Flooding occurred on the St Johns River on Thursday night August 21-22 in the Palatka area of Putnam County as northeasterly winds created a seiche effect driving river water southward.  On Friday morning August 22nd the winds became more southeasterly along the river and river water was driven north in the San Marco and Riverside areas of Downtown Jacksonville flooding roadways and some structures. River water depths of 4 to 6 inches flooded the Park Lane Condominium in Riverside. A Ford Bronco in the street outside of the condo floated in the river water a short distance into Memorial Park. Flash flooding occurred on McCoys Creek on Thursday night and Moncrief Creek on Friday. Both creeks are in Jacksonville, Duval County, Florida.  Several vehicles were stranded at Kings Road and McCoys Creek Blvd including one vehicle up to its roof. Areal flooding occurred over much of northeast Florida with roads and small creeks flooded. Minor flooding of structures occurred across the area. Numerous trees and power lines were downed with minor structural damage. High surf and beach erosion threatened five homes on the 3850 block of S Ponte Vedra Blvd in Vilano Beach, Florida. Several minor tornado touchdowns occurred in the area with no significant damage or injuries. Many north Florida creeks and Rivers experienced fresh water flooding with Black Creek, The St Marys River at McClenny, the St Johns River, south of Palatka, and the Sante Fe River experiencing the most significant flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay came ashore along the northeast Florida coast on the 21st.  Fay moved slowly westward across northern Florida, producing widespread heavy rainfall and strong winds. On the 22nd, Fay began to approach the waters of Apalachee Bay.  Fay emerged into Apalachee Bay late on the 22nd, and made its fourth Florida landfall on the 23rd near Carrabelle. By that morning, Fay had tapped into Gulf of Mexico moisture.  Intense rainbands developed and trained over the same locations in the Florida Big Bend for several hours on the 23rd producing rainfall totals exceeding 20 inches in some locations.  This resulted in record flooding on area rivers and flooding in Tallahassee and surrounding cities that has not been seen in many years.  By the 24th, Fay had weakened to a depression and continued moving westward into southeastern Mississippi.  Besides the torrential rainfall associated with Fay, sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph downed many trees and power lines.  Numerous homes were damaged by fallen trees as well as the tropical storm-force wind gusts.  A tornado briefly touced down in a wooded area of Jefferson County on the 23rd.  A Talquin Electric linesman was electrocuted while working to restore power in Quincy when a tree fell across a nearby power line.  A Jefferson County man lost control of his vehicle and crashed into a flooded wooded area and drowned.  A man was killed during a heavy rain storm in Bay County when he lost control of his vehicle and collided with a large pine tree.  A state of emergency was declared by the Governor for all but Walton County.  Individual assistance was granted for Gadsden, Jefferson, Leon, Liberty, Madison, Taylor and Wakulla counties.  Many county schools were closed on the 22nd and 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall from Fay varied from ten to 24 inches.  Numerous trees and power lines were down throughout the county.  Numerous state and county roads were closed by flood waters.  There were a few road collapses, which included a portion of Capital Circle NE just south of Mahan Drive.  The Timberlake subdivision east of Tallahassee was cut off by flood waters during the storm, which affected 230 homes.  The total number of homes affected by flooding countywide was 450.  Twenty homes were damaged and 14 were destroyed by fallen trees and flooding.  Flooding was observed along the Ochlockonee River and Munson Slough.  The crest at Havana was 33.71 feet, which was at major flood stage and 1.4 feet below the record flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure south of the area kept west to southwest winds in place. Deep moisture also in place allowed for numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms to move over the area from the Gulf of Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A television news helicopter reported the area around East Broadway Avenue and East Columbus Drive had flooding in parking lots, side streets and a few homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure ridging across the area allowed an afternoon sea breeze to develop with scattered thunderstorms forming along the boundary. Some drier air moved over the area aloft, allowing for a few severe thunderstorms containing hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured a 62 MPH wind gust from their home weather station anemometer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure ridging across the area allowed an afternoon sea breeze to develop with scattered thunderstorms forming along the boundary. Some drier air moved over the area aloft, allowing for a few severe thunderstorms containing hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail occurred for five minutes on the University of South Florida campus in Tampa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ike moved west to west-northwest across eastern Cuba, just offshore the south central coast, and then crossed through western Cuba into the Gulf of Mexico.  Tropical storm force winds and two tornadoes were reported in the Florida Keys. However, rainfall amounts and storm surge were minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm tides estimated near 2.0 feet above sea level combined with heavy wave action resulting in a closure of U.S. Highway 1 in Lower Matecumbe Key. Erosion occurred to the causeways surrounding the Card Sound Bridge, however most damages were the result of debris removal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Swells from Hurricane Ike caused above normal tides along the west coast of Florida. Ike made landfall in the early morning hours of September 13th near Galveston, TX.||In Pinellas County...the Shore Acres neighborhood in Saint Petersburg had several of its streets flooded from storm surge. Water was also over a dock in Saint Pete Beach and the road was flooded near the intersection of Carson Street and 40th Avenue in Saint Petersburg. A tide gauge at Clearwater Beach reported a maximum storm surge of 1.90 feet and a storm tide of 4.80 feet. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of roads in Holmes Beach was reported by emergency management.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure continued to ridge across the area with drier air in place aloft. Scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed with the sea breeze each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public estimated a wind gust of 60 MPH in Punta Gorda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea breeze thunderstorms produced strong winds in Central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a wind gust of 54 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was ridging across northern Florida keeping east to southeast winds in place across the local area. An afternoon sea breeze developed and combined with the east coast sea breeze to form several severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds around the Tampa Bay area in the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck an apartment building on the 6300 block of South Mac Dill Avenue and started a fire which heavily damaged two units. Water used to put out the fire damaged another two units.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was ridging across northern Florida keeping east to southeast winds in place across the local area. An afternoon sea breeze developed and combined with the east coast sea breeze to form several severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds around the Tampa Bay area in the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured a wind gust over 60 MPH near Tropicana Field with a hand-held anemometer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The high pressure ridge axis shifted south of the area keeping west to southwest winds in place across the area. Deep moisture also in place allowed for numerous thunderstorms producing heavy rains mainly across the northern Tampa Bay area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported nine inches of standing water and street flooding near Gulf Way and Sea Pines Drive in Hudson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The high pressure ridge axis shifted south of the area keeping west to southwest winds in place across the area. Deep moisture also in place allowed for numerous thunderstorms producing heavy rains mainly across the northern Tampa Bay area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pasco Fire Rescue reported street flooding with vehicles stranded in the water along State Road 54 in Seven Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure south of the area kept west to southwest winds in place. Deep moisture also in place allowed for numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms to move over the area from the Gulf of Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused localized street flooding with water up to the bottom of vehicles on 20th Street east of US Highway 301 and north of Chancey Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Canadian high pressure system built into the area behind a cold front. ||Levy County: The COOP station near Chiefland observed a record low temperature on the morning of October 29th with 28 degrees, beating the previous record of 34 degrees set in 1987 and 1968.||Citrus County: The COOP station near Inverness observed a record low temperature on the morning of October 29th with 31 degrees, beating the previous record of 42 degrees set in 1910.||Sumter County:  The COOP station near Bushnell observed a record low temperature on the morning of October 29th with 33 degrees, beating the previous record of 35 degrees set in 1943.||Pasco County:  The COOP station near St. Leo observed a record low temperature on the morning of October 29th with 40 degrees, beating the previous record of 41 degrees set in 1910.||Pinellas County:  The ASOS station at the Albert Whitted Airport in St. Petersburg observed a record low maximum temperature on October 28th of 63 degrees, beating the previous low maximum temperature of 67 degrees set in 1997. The low temperature on the morning of October 29th was just above the record 49 degrees set in 1952, falling to 51 degrees. Wednesday afternoon then tied the record low maximum temperature of 64, also set in 1952.||Hillsborough County: The ASOS station at the Tampa International Airport observed a record low maximum temperature on October 28th of 64 degrees, beating the previous low maximum temperature of 65 degrees set in 1892. The low temperature on the morning of October 29th was also a record 42 degrees, beating the previous record of 44 degrees set in 1910. The COOP Station in Plant City also tied the record low with 38 degrees, also set in 1892.||Polk County:  The COOP station near Lakeland observed a record low temperature on the morning of October 29th with 38 degrees, beating the previous record of 48 degrees set in 1957. The COOP station near Lake Wales also had a record low temperature of 36 degrees, beating the previous record of 38 degrees set in 1943.||Manatee County:  The ASOS station at the Sarasota-Bradenton International Airport observed a record low maximum temperature on October 28th of 64 degrees, beating the previous low maximum temperature of 70 degrees set in 1957. The low temperature on the morning of October 29th was a record 44 degrees, beating the previous record of 45 degrees set in 1957. Wednesday afternoon then again saw record low maximum temperatures, with a high of 65 beating the previous record low maximum of 69 set in 1997.||Sarasota County:  The COOP station near Venice observed a record low temperature on the morning of October 29th with 42 degrees, beating the previous record of 46 degrees set in 1957. The COOP station near the Myakka River State Park also had a record low temperature of 37 degrees, beating the previous record of 46 degrees set in 1957.||DeSoto County:  The COOP station near Arcadia observed a record low temperature on the morning of October 29th with 38 degrees, beating the previous record of 39 degrees set in 1944.||Highlands County:  The COOP station near Avon Park observed a record low temperature on the morning of October 29th with 39 degrees, beating the previous record of 40 degrees set in 1943. The COOP station in Archbold also had a record low temperature of 35 degrees, beating the previous record of 44 degrees set in 2000.||Lee County:  The ASOS station at Page Field in Fort Myers observed a record low maximum temperature on October 28th of 67 degrees, beating the previous low maximum temperature of 70 degrees set in 1937. The low temperature on the morning of October 29th then tied the record of 47 degrees set in 1910.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station near Bushnell observed a record low temperature on the morning of October 29th with 33 degrees, beating the previous record of 35 degrees set in 1943.
