EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A morning waterspout on the 8th came ashore as a EF0 tornado in the Bay Point/Preserve subdivision and again at Port Panama City.  Late in the afternoon, an EF1 tornado touched down a few miles southwest of Lloyd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout formed over the Grand Lagoon and came ashore as a tornado in the Bay Point/Preserve subdivision.  Windows and roofs of several homes were damaged, a chimney was blown off a roof, and several trees and power lines were toppled.  The tornado reentered the bay and came ashore near the Port of Panama City.  Before it dissipated, tt tore off the roof of a building at 23rd Street and Grant Avenue and damaged a mobile home in the 2500 block of Grant Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A morning waterspout on the 8th came ashore as a EF0 tornado in the Bay Point/Preserve subdivision and again at Port Panama City.  Late in the afternoon, an EF1 tornado touched down a few miles southwest of Lloyd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down along Robert Thompson Road.  Large pine trees were snapped, with several uprooted.  A 12 by 12 foot gazebo was destroyed.  The tornado weakened as it moved quickly to the north and dissipated before it crossed Whitehouse Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tides running two feet above normal astronomical tide at Alligator Point caused minor coastal flooding along the lower portion of Alligator Point Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved east across north Florida over night on Wednesday 12/10 and during the day on Thursday 12/11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved across north Florida and into the Magaretta area. As the system moved into the area of Mack Ruise Road and Ruise Lane a microburst impacted the area with an estimated 60 to 75 mph sustained winds. One mobile home had a hole punched through its roof. A second structure, built largely of particle board with no reinforcing bracing, was unroofed with portions of the roof thrown approximately 100 yards to the north. The damage made this structure uninhabitable and it was the most severely impacted structure. A third structure had its sheet metal roof removed from the southern portion of the structure and thrown to the north. Two trees were snapped at the base and laid flat to the north. A witness described seeing a vortex and a second witness described being lifted off the ground by that vortex. These reports are consistent with a possible gustnado and it is believed that this is what contacted the most severely affected structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ike passed across Cuba and the southwestern Gulf of Mexico during September 9th and 10th producing tropical storm conditions in portions of south Florida. The system produced wind gusts of up to 63 MPH and generally around 6 inches of rain or less across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of South Florida experienced tropical storm conditions while Hurricane Ike passed across Cuba and the southwestern Gulf of Mexico during September 9th and 10th. The center of hurricane at its closest to mainland south Florida remained around 200 miles away as a category one with sustained winds around 75 MPH and a minimum central pressure of 970 MB. Tropical storm conditions were mainly felt across the far southern tip of the mainland and along the southwest coast including the counties of Miami-Dade and mainland sections of Monroe. The highest wind gust recorded was 63 MPH at Flamingo along with a sustained wind of 52 MPH. This occurred as a rain band swept across south Florida. In Miami-Dade County, the highest sustained wind occurred at Virginia Key at 41 MPH, and this occurred with a gust of 51 MPH. The Opa Locka Airport also reported tropical storm conditions with a sustained wind of 39 MPH along with a wind gust of 46 MPH. The wind caused around 6000 people to lose power during the storm in Miami-Dade County, but no impact of note occurred elsewhere.||Rainfall amounts where highest along the southwest coast in Collier and Monroe Counties with the highest rainfall totals ranging from 6.33 inches at Ochopee to 2.55 inches a few miles northeast of Golden Gate Estates.||Storm Surge associated with Ike was minimal across mainland south Florida with the highest reported at Naples. The highest storm tide in south Florida was 3.15 feet with a surge of 2.23 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Gustav moved south of northwest Florida tropical storm force winds were observed along the coastal areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As hurricane Gustav moved south of the area on September 1, tropical storm force winds were observed along the immediate coast and also offshore.  A tropical storm warning that was issued for northwest Florida the end of August, continued into the first of September.  The warning was dropped at 900 CST on September 1.  Tropical storm force winds were measured at a mesonet site along the coast operated by Eglin Air Force Base.  Highest sustained winds were measured at 43 MPG with a peak gust of 51 MPH during the morning hours.  Higher than normal tides along the beaches of northwest Florida caused beach erosion.  Water also washed across some of the dunes and caused flooding in some roads and also under some condos.  Damage estimates from the surge were $550K.  Six weak tornadoes were reported along the northwest Florida coast.  No major damages were reported from the tornadoes with damage estimates of $200K.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 19th Tropical Storm Fay came on shore in south west Florida moving north northeast toward Lake Okeechobee. By the early morning of August 20th Tropical Storm Fay had moved to southern Brevard County producing rainfall amounts ranging from 10 to 15 inches in Martin, Saint Lucie, Okeechobee, and Indian River counties. Okeechobee reported 69 homes damaged by flood waters and a total of $1.2 million. Indian River and St. Lucie counties reported a combined loss in citrus of $10 million. One indirect fatality occurred in Indian River County when a car hydroplaned and crashed.

Tropical Storm Fay moved slowly north out of Caribbean Sea, then cut across south and central Florida into the Atlantic Ocean. Fay then slowly curved west and made landfall in the vicinity for Flagler Beach and Daytona Beach on Aug 21st. As the storm made landfall sustained winds along the coast were between 40 and 50 mph with gusts in the 60 to 65 mph range. Storm Surge ranged from 2 to 4 feet along the open coast and about 1 to 2 feet on the St Johns River.  Storm Surge Flooding occurred on the St Johns River on Thursday night August 21-22 in the Palatka area of Putnam County as northeasterly winds created a seiche effect driving river water southward.  On Friday morning August 22nd the winds became more southeasterly along the river and river water was driven north in the San Marco and Riverside areas of Downtown Jacksonville flooding roadways and some structures. River water depths of 4 to 6 inches flooded the Park Lane Condominium in Riverside. A Ford Bronco in the street outside of the condo floated in the river water a short distance into Memorial Park. Flash flooding occurred on McCoys Creek on Thursday night and Moncrief Creek on Friday. Both creeks are in Jacksonville, Duval County, Florida.  Several vehicles were stranded at Kings Road and McCoys Creek Blvd including one vehicle up to its roof. Areal flooding occurred over much of northeast Florida with roads and small creeks flooded. Minor flooding of structures occurred across the area. Numerous trees and power lines were downed with minor structural damage. High surf and beach erosion threatened five homes on the 3850 block of S Ponte Vedra Blvd in Vilano Beach, Florida. Several minor tornado touchdowns occurred in the area with no significant damage or injuries. Many north Florida creeks and Rivers experienced fresh water flooding with Black Creek, The St Marys River at McClenny, the St Johns River, south of Palatka, and the Sante Fe River experiencing the most significant flooding.

The high tide forced residents of a condo on Pensacola Beach to move to higher floors as water splashed over the dunes and covered part of the lower floor of the condo. The man���s body was later retrieved at the  Commercial Boulevard Pier which is south of where the man first entered the water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay came ashore along the northeast Florida coast on the 21st.  Fay moved slowly westward across northern Florida, producing widespread heavy rainfall and strong winds. On the 22nd, Fay began to approach the waters of Apalachee Bay.  Fay emerged into Apalachee Bay late on the 22nd, and made its fourth Florida landfall on the 23rd near Carrabelle. By that morning, Fay had tapped into Gulf of Mexico moisture.  Intense rainbands developed and trained over the same locations in the Florida Big Bend for several hours on the 23rd producing rainfall totals exceeding 20 inches in some locations.  This resulted in record flooding on area rivers and flooding in Tallahassee and surrounding cities that has not been seen in many years.  By the 24th, Fay had weakened to a depression and continued moving westward into southeastern Mississippi.  Besides the torrential rainfall associated with Fay, sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph downed many trees and power lines.  Numerous homes were damaged by fallen trees as well as the tropical storm-force wind gusts.  A tornado briefly touced down in a wooded area of Jefferson County on the 23rd.  A Talquin Electric linesman was electrocuted while working to restore power in Quincy when a tree fell across a nearby power line.  A Jefferson County man lost control of his vehicle and crashed into a flooded wooded area and drowned.  A man was killed during a heavy rain storm in Bay County when he lost control of his vehicle and collided with a large pine tree.  A state of emergency was declared by the Governor for all but Walton County.  Individual assistance was granted for Gadsden, Jefferson, Leon, Liberty, Madison, Taylor and Wakulla counties.  Many county schools were closed on the 22nd and 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall from Fay varied between six and 14 inches.  There were numerous downed trees and power lines throughout the county.  Fifteen single family homes and six mobile homes were damaged by fallen trees and flooding.  Several county roads were impassable due to debris, with some dirt roads undermined by flood waters.  U.S. Highway 90 at the Little River and U.S. Highway 27 at Interstate 10 were closed due to flooding.

In Miami-Dade County, the highest sustained wind occurred at Virginia Key at 41 MPH with a gust of 51 MPH. The Opa Locka Airport also reported tropical storm conditions with a sustained wind of 39 MPH along with a wind gust of 46 MPH. The wind caused around 6000 people to lose power during the storm in Miami-Dade County, but no impacts of note occurred elsewhere.||Rainfall amounts where highest along the southwest coast in Collier and Monroe Counties with the highest rainfall totals ranging from 6.33 inches at Ochopee to 2.55 inches a few miles northeast of Golden Gate Estates.||Storm Surge associated with Ike was minimal across mainland south Florida with the highest reported at Naples. The highest storm tide in south Florida was 3.15 feet with a surge of 2.23 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms over metro Miami-Dade County on September 15th led to lightning striking a construction crane in western Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a construction crane in western Miami-Dade County while 2 workers were on board.  Both workers were taken to the hospital after the bolt travel down the metal crane and were in good condition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 21 year old male was caught in a rip current while swimming on Fort Lauderdale Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 21 year old male was swimming with a group of friends on Fort Lauderdale beach during the afternoon of Sunday, September 14th when he was caught in a rip current. Attempts were made by friends to pull him out of the current, but were unsuccessful. The coast guard conducted a search and rescue through the night and into Monday afternoon, but could not find the victim. The victim washed up on the shore along highway A1A early Wednesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay moved slowly north out of Caribbean Sea, then cut across south and central Florida into the Atlantic Ocean. Fay then slowly curved west and made landfall in the vicinity for Flagler Beach and Daytona Beach on Aug 21st. As the storm made landfall sustained winds along the coast were between 40 and 50 mph with gusts in the 60 to 65 mph range. Storm Surge ranged from 2 to 4 feet along the open coast and about 1 to 2 feet on the St Johns River.  Storm Surge Flooding occurred on the St Johns River on Thursday night August 21-22 in the Palatka area of Putnam County as northeasterly winds created a seiche effect driving river water southward.  On Friday morning August 22nd the winds became more southeasterly along the river and river water was driven north in the San Marco and Riverside areas of Downtown Jacksonville flooding roadways and some structures. River water depths of 4 to 6 inches flooded the Park Lane Condominium in Riverside. A Ford Bronco in the street outside of the condo floated in the river water a short distance into Memorial Park. Flash flooding occurred on McCoys Creek on Thursday night and Moncrief Creek on Friday. Both creeks are in Jacksonville, Duval County, Florida.  Several vehicles were stranded at Kings Road and McCoys Creek Blvd including one vehicle up to its roof. Areal flooding occurred over much of northeast Florida with roads and small creeks flooded. Minor flooding of structures occurred across the area. Numerous trees and power lines were downed with minor structural damage. High surf and beach erosion threatened five homes on the 3850 block of S Ponte Vedra Blvd in Vilano Beach, Florida. Several minor tornado touchdowns occurred in the area with no significant damage or injuries. Many north Florida creeks and Rivers experienced fresh water flooding with Black Creek, The St Marys River at McClenny, the St Johns River, south of Palatka, and the Sante Fe River experiencing the most significant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: McCoys Creek flooded to near record levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thundestorms developed along the east coast sea breeze on the afternoon of September 29th, and a funnel cloud was spotted in South Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from the weather observer at Homestead Air Reserve Base, trained spotters and an off duty National Weather Service employee indicated a funnel cloud in the Goulds and Princeton area of South Miami-Dade County. The funnel cloud was visible for several minutes as the parent storm was nearly stationary, but did not touch down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ike passed across Cuba and the southwestern Gulf of Mexico during September 9th and 10th producing tropical storm conditions in portions of south Florida. The system produced wind gusts of up to 63 MPH and generally around 6 inches of rain or less across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of South Florida experienced tropical storm conditions while Hurricane Ike passed across Cuba and the southwestern Gulf of Mexico during September 9th and 10th. The center of hurricane at its closest to mainland south Florida remained around 200 miles away as a category one with sustained winds around 75 MPH and a minimum central pressure of 970 MB. Tropical storm conditions were mainly felt across the far southern tip of the mainland and along the southwest coast including the counties of Miami-Dade and mainland sections of Monroe. The highest wind gust recorded was 63 MPH at Flamingo along with a sustained wind of 52 MPH. This occurred as a rain band swept across south Florida. In Miami-Dade County, the highest sustained wind occurred at Virginia Key at 41 MPH, and this occurred with a gust of 51 MPH. The Opa Locka Airport also reported tropical storm conditions with a sustained wind of 39 MPH along with a wind gust of 46 MPH. The wind caused around 6000 people to lose power during the storm in Miami-Dade County, but no impact of note occurred elsewhere.||Rainfall amounts where highest along the southwest coast in Collier and Monroe Counties with the highest rainfall totals ranging from 6.33 inches at Ochopee to 2.55 inches a few miles northeast of Golden Gate Estates.||Storm Surge associated with Ike was minimal across mainland south Florida with the highest reported at Naples. The highest storm tide in south Florida was 3.15 feet with a surge of 2.23 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay came ashore along the northeast Florida coast on the 21st.  Fay moved slowly westward across northern Florida, producing widespread heavy rainfall and strong winds. On the 22nd, Fay began to approach the waters of Apalachee Bay.  Fay emerged into Apalachee Bay late on the 22nd, and made its fourth Florida landfall on the 23rd near Carrabelle. By that morning, Fay had tapped into Gulf of Mexico moisture.  Intense rainbands developed and trained over the same locations in the Florida Big Bend for several hours on the 23rd producing rainfall totals exceeding 20 inches in some locations.  This resulted in record flooding on area rivers and flooding in Tallahassee and surrounding cities that has not been seen in many years.  By the 24th, Fay had weakened to a depression and continued moving westward into southeastern Mississippi.  Besides the torrential rainfall associated with Fay, sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph downed many trees and power lines.  Numerous homes were damaged by fallen trees as well as the tropical storm-force wind gusts.  A tornado briefly touced down in a wooded area of Jefferson County on the 23rd.  A Talquin Electric linesman was electrocuted while working to restore power in Quincy when a tree fell across a nearby power line.  A Jefferson County man lost control of his vehicle and crashed into a flooded wooded area and drowned.  A man was killed during a heavy rain storm in Bay County when he lost control of his vehicle and collided with a large pine tree.  A state of emergency was declared by the Governor for all but Walton County.  Individual assistance was granted for Gadsden, Jefferson, Leon, Liberty, Madison, Taylor and Wakulla counties.  Many county schools were closed on the 22nd and 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall from Fay varied between two and three inches.  There were several reports of downed trees and power lines throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving warm front over extreme southeast Florida produced an area of very heavy rainfall over much of the region. Many areas received minor flooding, and a few areas received more significant ponding of water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of heavy rain continued to slowly move south to southeast out of Broward County into Miami-Dade County with rainfall estimates from the National Weather Service radar at two to three inches per hour. On Miami Beach, an off-duty weather service employee reported water entering cars, while more reports of the same were received from Key Biscayne. Several roads were closed with 2 to 3 feet of water for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving warm front over extreme southeast Florida produced an area of very heavy rainfall over much of the region. Many areas received minor flooding, and a few areas received more significant ponding of water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of heavy rainfall produced flash flooding across portions of southeastern Broward County, primarily in the Hollywood, Pembroke Pines and Cooper City areas. North Perry Airport received 4.95 inches in 2 �� hours. An off-duty National Weather Service employee reported 3 feet of water over the intersection of Johnson Street and University Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong east to southeast winds of 20 to 25 mph caused strong rip currents to form along the southeast Florida Atlantic beaches. A rip current in Hollywood claimed the life of a 57 year old man.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 57 year old male was swimming with a friend and his son at an unguarded beach near North Ocean Drive and Forrest Street in Hollywood Beach when a rip current caused him to drown. Medics performed CPR but were unable to save him.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ike moved west to west-northwest across eastern Cuba, just offshore the south central coast, and then crossed through western Cuba into the Gulf of Mexico.  Tropical storm force winds and two tornadoes were reported in the Florida Keys. However, rainfall amounts and storm surge were minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ike passed well southwest of the Florida Keys and into the Gulf of Mexico. However, Ike's large wind field produced tropical storm force winds during September 9-10, 2008.  Storm total rainfall was highest in the Monroe County Lower Keys at Key West International Airport, where 2.31 inches was recorded.  The highest sustained wind of 45 knots with gusts to 52 knots was recorded at Key West International Airport. A peak gust of 57 knots was recorded at the NWS office in Key West.  The lowest sea-level pressure of 1004.4 mb was recorded at Key West International Airport.  Coastal storm tides measured at up to 2.2 feet above sea level at Key West Harbor, equating to about 1.2 feet of storm surge. No fatalities or injuries were reported in the Lower Keys of Monroe County. Mandatory evacuations for residents and visitors were ordered from September 6 through 7, 2008. Total damage to government property in Monroe County was estimated at $13.5 million dollars.  Approximately $1.2 million damage in debris removal and erosion occurred county-wide due to storm surge flooding, with $2.6 million estimated from combined effects from wind and wind-blown rain.  Of particular note was an estimated $7 million loss to the commercial lobster fishery due to lost and relocated traps.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ike moved west to west-northwest across eastern Cuba, just offshore the south central coast, and then crossed through western Cuba into the Gulf of Mexico.  Tropical storm force winds and two tornadoes were reported in the Florida Keys. However, rainfall amounts and storm surge were minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm tides up to 1.2 feet above sea level were measured at Vaca Key, equating to a storm surge value of 1.0 feet.  A storm tide of 1.7 feet above sea level was measured offshore at Sombrero Key. Storm tides were estimated up to 2 feet above sea level along the south shore of Marathon, resulting in minor road flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ike moved west to west-northwest across eastern Cuba, just offshore the south central coast, and then crossed through western Cuba into the Gulf of Mexico.  Tropical storm force winds and two tornadoes were reported in the Florida Keys. However, rainfall amounts and storm surge were minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm tides up to 2.5 feet above sea level were estimated along the south shore of Key West, resulting in numerous flooded streets and a few road closures. The maximum storm surge of 1.7 feet due to Hurricane Ike occurred approximately 8 hours earlier during low astronomical tide.  Key West Harbor recorded a storm tide of 2.2 feet above sea level, equating to a subsiding storm surge value of 1.2 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ike moved west to west-northwest across eastern Cuba, just offshore the south central coast, and then crossed through western Cuba into the Gulf of Mexico.  Tropical storm force winds and two tornadoes were reported in the Florida Keys. However, rainfall amounts and storm surge were minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A violent waterspout associated with a supercell made landfall as a tornado in Lower Matecumbe Key at Sunset Drive and moved northwest, crossing U.S. Highway One and then along Palm Drive through a residential section near Mile Marker 74 on the Overseas Highway. The tornado damage path ended on Buttonwood Lane before reaching Florida Bay. Most damage was to deck railings, screens, windows and soffits on numerous homes. A few homes displayed more significant damage, including penetration through exterior doors and walls, with one home losing 50 percent of its roof decking. The EF1 rating with estimated maximum wind speeds of 90 to 100 mph was based on roof damage to a residence along Palm Drive. Debris from sheet metal paneling and mangrove branches may have contributed to a major power outage throughout all of the Keys from Islamorada through Key West as it was carried across U.S. Highway One and a major electrical transmission line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ike moved west to west-northwest across eastern Cuba, just offshore the south central coast, and then crossed through western Cuba into the Gulf of Mexico.  Tropical storm force winds and two tornadoes were reported in the Florida Keys. However, rainfall amounts and storm surge were minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado touched down on Atlantic side of U.S. Highway One and moved toward the northwest. The tornado caused minor damage to facia on a business on the bayside of U.S. Highway One, then moved into a residential area. One residence with two broken windows, and another residence had an exterior wall penetrated with a 2x4 carried from over 250 yards away from a home under construction. Several trees and large limbs downed. Estimated maximum wind speed 60 to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure remained in place across southern Florida with west to southwest winds aloft. Afternoon sea breezes developed each day and thunderstorms formed along sea breeze and outflow boundaries. On June 23, one person was killed and 2 people were injured by lightning while seeking refuge under a tree in DeSoto County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 28 year old man was struck and injured by lightning while wade fishing in the Gulf off of Siesta Key.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure remained in place across southern Florida with west to southwest winds aloft. EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 4:30 p.m. EDT on June 23, 2008, Employee #1 was working at a ranch, clipping dead limbs off of a palm tree, when a heavy rain and lightning approached. Employee #1 and two coworkers sought refuge under a large 30-ft oak tree. While under the tree, Employee #1 leaned up against the tree. When lightning struck the tree, Employee #1 sustained burns to his hands and feet and was killed. Both coworkers were airlifted to Tampa General Hospital in Tampa, Florida.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fourty four mobile homes had minor damage to roofs, carports and lanais. Ten mobile homes had moderate damage to roofs and portions of walls. One un-anchored single-wide mobile home was pushed 5 feet from its original position, with the roof blown off and exterior walls partially collapsed. One double-wide mobile home had complete structural failure of a well-constructed outer wall and roof section. Most of the damage was in the high-end EF0 range, with a small pocket of low-end EF1 damage. Wind speeds were estimated between 75-100mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough over southeast Georgia sagged southward toward north Florida and served as a focus for heavy rainfall from storms in the evening that fired along the sea breezes and outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Craig Airfield ASOS recorded 2.53 inches of rainfall since 12 pm EDT. It measured a 24-hour total of 4.27 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon sea breeze triggered thunderstorms over Central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF0 tornado touched down blowing down trees and damaging the roofs of two mobile homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and evening thunderstorms along the southwest Florida gulf coast caused damage to a home in Marco Island as a result of a lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a 5 foot concrete wall in front of a Marco Island residence, blowing out 60 to 70 pound chunks of the wall. Some of the concrete traveled about 40 feet into the neighbor���s yard and also into the front of the house. Holes were gouged in the front door. Two gas lamps along the driveway also exploded leaving a hole about a foot in diameter. The bolt also caused damage to the home���s electronics. Total estimated cost of the damage to the concrete wall, lamps, front door, garage, and electronics was around $75,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and squall line transited north Florida on Tuesday, February 26th, 2008.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AW portion of metal out building, under construction, was blown over at 44205 mcKendree Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief, but extensive severe weather episode occurred across the Florida Big Bend during the morning of March 7, 2008. There were widespread reports of straight line wind damage across the area, with two confirmed tornadoes reported by NWS survey teams. There was also some urban street flooding in Tallahassee.  Nearly 10,000 Tallahassee customers were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were some downed trees and power lines from just east of Cherry Lake to near Pinetta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief, but extensive severe weather episode occurred across the Florida Big Bend during the morning of March 7, 2008. There were widespread reports of straight line wind damage across the area, with two confirmed tornadoes reported by NWS survey teams. There was also some urban street flooding in Tallahassee.  Nearly 10,000 Tallahassee customers were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just west of Capitola near Capitola and Baum Roads, and downed trees and power lines.  It then traveled northeast across Capitola and severely damaged eight homes on Whitehouse and Capitola Roads.  One person was injured and transported to a local hospital.  Downed trees and power lines closed Whitehouse Road at Eva Road, Bucklake Road at Epping Forest Road, Dayflower Circle at Blackberry Lane, and Jefferson Road at Roalde Road.  Several homes were damaged with downed trees and power lines on Turkey Roost Road just north of Interstate 10.  The tornado lifted just northeast of Wadesboro near Wadesboro Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The daily east coast sea breeze pushed inland and produced thunderstorms over coastal and interior sections of Central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down on Citrus Boulevard. .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon sea breeze remained close to the coast producing wide spread heavy rain, hail and thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: St. Lucie County Emergency Management reported a funnel cloud sighting on Jenkins Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Morning soundings indicated a moist and unstable airmass over the local area with low wind shear and a light southwest steering flow. The precipitable water was 1.76 inches at Jacksonville with around 3000 J/kg of CAPE and a lifted index of -6 degrees Celcius. An upper level low pressure system approached the eastern seaboard during the afternoon which pushed a surface trough over Georgia. This trough in concert with the sea breezes sparked numerous severe storms across the local forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was blown down east of Hastings along County Road 13 South.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prevailing moist west flow brought numerous mainly afternoon showers and thunderstorms to the Atlantic coast. Storm motion was less than 10 knots and precipitable water values were over 2 inches with very high low level instability index values. These ingredients supported heavy rainfall in convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 1.5 inches fell in 20 minutes around 3 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed along the inland-pushing sea breeze over the city of Hialeah. Favorable wind profiles along the sea breeze front helped to produce a brief tornado that damaged 8 structures. Other storms which formed along the sea breeze front in Palm Beach County caused  lightning damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a Royal Palm complex setting ablaze to a few condominiums. The fire forced residents out of six units as fire spread through the attic and water from the hoses was pumped in damaging lower floors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late night thunderstorms brought hail, intense lightning, strong winds and several reports of funnel clouds across southern Bay County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several public sightings of a funnel cloud along Front Beach Road near Panama City Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The high pressure ridge axis shifted south of the area keeping west to southwest winds in place across the area. Deep moisture also in place allowed for numerous thunderstorms producing heavy rains mainly across the northern Tampa Bay area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 4.50 inches of rain in ten hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line with severe weather and several tornadoes moved across north Florida and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail reported by park official who lives on the Silver River State Park property. Time based on radar imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea breeze thunderstorms produced heavy rain and some hail in Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed over the Gulf of Mexico and moved across northern Florida, pushing a strong cold front through the area in its wake. A broad line of rapidly moving thunderstorms brought thunderstorm wind damage across the northern half of the local area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home weather station measured sustained winds of 61 MPH and a wind gust of 73 MPH. The wind uprooted pine trees which tore down power lines and blocked traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay emerged into the Apalachee Bay late Friday night and made its fourth landfall on Saturday, August 23rd near Carrabelle, Florida.  That morning, Fay had tapped into Gulf of Mexico moisture.  A large feeder band began affecting areas east of the Apalachicola River during the morning, followed by more intense bands, which trained over the same locations during the afternoon and evening hours, producing extremely high rainfall rates and subsequent flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads and low lying areas were flooded, including Mayo and Buckville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and squall line transited north Florida on Tuesday, February 26th, 2008.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reports a tree down at 5415 North Oceanshore Blvd in Hammock. the time was adjusted based upon radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convective complex over Georgia initiated by a passing upper level short wave early in the day produced a large scale outflow boundary that advanced over our zones during the afternoon and early evening hours over a moist and unstable airmass. Outflows combined with a strong sea breeze collision near the east coast ignited scattered severe storms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size was reported at the Spruce Creek Preserve east of Dunnellon. Rainfall rates were near 2 inches per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level low enhanced sea breeze and outflow initiated convection over an unstable and moist airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail was reported near the Target store and Interstate 75.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level low enhanced sea breeze and outflow initiated convection over an unstable and moist airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 21 year old male was struck by lightning near 3500 SW Archer Road. He was rushed to Shands Hospital. The victim was standing under a tree before he was struck.  He suffered extreme burns to half of his body.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level low enhanced sea breeze and outflow initiated convection over an unstable and moist airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was down off of Longbranch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward moving boundary produced severe thunderstorms across central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust to 60 mph was recorded. A large tree also fell on a pool enclosure in Kissimmee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon sea breeze triggered thunderstorms over Central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Orlando Fire Department reported a fire truck hit by a falling tree during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north of Florida early in the day which increased surface based instability and low level moisture. A passing upper level short wave trough and sea breeze driven outflows boundaries enhanced convection across the state during the late afternoon and evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An evening thunderstorm on the 12th caused wind damage east of Crawfordville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure was in place across the southern half of the area with upper impulses moving over the area. Numerous showers and scattered thunderstorms formed and moved over the area in this unstable environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a pine tree in the Fort Myers Villas neighborhood. The strike scorched the tree and scattered debris over fifty feet away, breaking windows on at least five nearby homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay produced only one recorded tornado in west central and southwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado formed over Lake Rosalie, moved onshore into Camp Rosalie, then dissipated.  Five structures in the camp were damaged by the tornado. Car ports and mobile home roofs were peeled back in places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary was positioned over Southeast Georgia with a moist and unstable airmass across the local area in advance of the front. Precipitable water amounts of 2.12 inches were in place and very high surface based instability index values with CAPEs around 3500 J/kg and lifted index values of -9 to -10.  Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the area during the afternoon and evening along the approaching front , sea breezes and old outflow boundaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Structural damage occurred to a residential home. The roof was partially damaged and a front porch destroyed by thunderstorm wind gusts. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure offshore of the Florida Atlantic coast produced a couple days of heavy rainfall across coastal northeast Florida as bands of convection around the low rotated inland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 4.3 inches was measured from July 16th through July 17th in Palm Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary remained across South Florida for a second day as another short wave moved across the region during the afternoon hours allowing severe thunderstorms to develop over Palm Beach County. The storms produced wind damage and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved across coastal sections of Palm Beach County and over the Lake Clarke Shores area downing power lines onto Forest Hill Boulevard and also downing a fence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe Thunderstorm moved from interior sections of South Florida, across portions of the eastern metro areas and into the Atlantic Ocean producing hail, wind and lightning damage, and waterspouts. An estimated 12,000 customers lost power in Palm Beach County during the episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm that developed over the Loxahatchee Wildlife Refuge moved northeast through metropolitan sections of Palm Beach County producing high wind gusts and damage. A Publix supermarket sign was blown down and moved 50 yards onto Military Trail from its original location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large outflow boundary from strong thunderstorms moved north across the Florida Straits the morning of October 22, 2008.  The east end of the boundary became nearly stationary across Key West, causing new thunderstorms to develop and train over the island.  Extensive street flooding occurred in Key West due to heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused street flooding one foot deep at Bertha and Dennis Streets on the southside of Key West.  The street was impassable to small cars and scooters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure south of the area kept west to southwest winds in place. Deep moisture also in place allowed for numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms to move over the area from the Gulf of Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter living along the south bank of the Orange River in Buckingham reported the river was rising rapidly from the previous day's rains and a barn was flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fisherman was struck and injured while standing on the beach at Indian River Shores. He was transported to a local hospital.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: FHP reported that Interstate 275 at Exit 42, Howard-Armenia, was blocked by 3 feet of water for an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad surface trough was nearly stationary over the forecast area as a short wave upper level trough traversed the region during the afternoon when instability was high. Widespread severe storms developed along the sea breezes and outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported penny size hail along Higway 218 and Blanding Blvd. There was also minor street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay affected South Florida on the 18th and 19th of August. The storm made landfall early in the morning of the 19th near Cape Romano, and moved over inland sections of South Florida during the morning and afternoon hours of the 19th, exiting the area near Lake Okeechobee. Fay caused tropical storm force winds, significant rainfall flooding in some areas, as well as two confirmed tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small and brief tornado tracked across Hallandale Beach south of Hallandale Beach Boulevard. Damage to patio screen enclosure, aluminum boats flipped, and trees and tree branches downed. Highest estimated wind was around 65 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay affected South Florida on the 18th and 19th of August. The storm made landfall early in the morning of the 19th near Cape Romano, and moved over inland sections of South Florida during the morning and afternoon hours of the 19th, exiting the area near Lake Okeechobee. Fay caused tropical storm force winds, significant rainfall flooding in some areas, as well as two confirmed tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts ranged 7 to 10 inches over northern Collier, Hendry and Glades counties near the path of the center of Fay. Isolated areas in northeastern Hendry and southeastern Glades counties received well in excess of 10 inches, with Moore Haven recorded the highest storm total rainfall of 16.17 inches recorded in the 48-hour period ending at 8 AM on Wednesday, August 20th.  Extensive flooding was observed over the areas with the highest totals, especially over Hendry and Glades counties. Hardest hit areas were the Felda and Montura areas in Hendry County, and the Muse and Moore Haven areas in Glades counties. Dozens of homes sustained damage from water intrusion, primarily in the Muse community and some roadways were closed for a few days. Nearly 40 homes were flooded in Glades County with most water intrusion occurring in the Muse area.
